The **Napoleonic Wars** (1803–1815) were a [global](/wiki/World_war "World war") series of conflicts fought by a fluctuating array of [European coalitions](/wiki/Coalition_forces_of_the_Napoleonic_Wars "Coalition forces of the Napoleonic Wars") against the [French First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic") (1803–1804) under the [First Consul](/wiki/French_Consulate "French Consulate") followed by the [First French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") (1804–1815) under the [Emperor of the French](/wiki/Emperor_of_the_French "Emperor of the French"), [Napoleon I](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"). The wars originated in political forces arising from the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") (1789–1799) and from the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") (1792–1802) and produced a period of French domination over [Continental Europe](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe").[[35]](#cite_note-55)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] The wars are categorised as seven conflicts, five named after the coalitions that fought Napoleon, plus two named for their respective theatres: the [War of the Third Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Third_Coalition "War of the Third Coalition"), [War of the Fourth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Fourth_Coalition "War of the Fourth Coalition"), [War of the Fifth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Fifth_Coalition "War of the Fifth Coalition"), [War of the Sixth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Sixth_Coalition "War of the Sixth Coalition"), [War of the Seventh Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Seventh_Coalition "War of the Seventh Coalition"), the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War"), and the [French invasion of Russia](/wiki/French_invasion_of_Russia "French invasion of Russia").[[36]](#cite_note-56)

The first stage of the war broke out when [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") declared war on France on 18 May 1803. After some minor campaigns, Britain allied with [Austria](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"), Russia, and several minor powers to form the Third Coalition in April 1805. Napoleon defeated the allied [Russo](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire")-[Austrian](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") armies in the subsequent war which climaxed in French victories at [Ulm](/wiki/Ulm_campaign "Ulm campaign") and [at Austerlitz](/wiki/Battle_of_Austerlitz "Battle of Austerlitz"), leading to the dissolution of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") and Austria being forced to make peace by the end of the year. Britain and Russia remained at war with France. Concerned about increasing French power, [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") joined Britain and Russia in the Fourth Coalition, which resumed war in October 1806. Napoleon defeated the Prussians [at Jena-Auerstedt](/wiki/Battle_of_Jena%E2%80%93Auerstedt "Battle of Jena–Auerstedt") and the Russians [at Friedland](/wiki/Battle_of_Friedland "Battle of Friedland"), bringing an [uneasy peace](/wiki/Treaties_of_Tilsit "Treaties of Tilsit") to the continent by July 1807, and again leaving Britain as France's sole major enemy. Britain was unable to dispute French dominance on the continent but obtained hegemony over the seas after a string of victories including [Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar"). Russia used the interim peace to resolve wars with the [Ottomans](/wiki/Russo-Turkish_War_(1806%E2%80%931812) "Russo-Turkish War (1806–1812)"), [Swedes](/wiki/Finnish_War "Finnish War"), and [Iranians.](/wiki/Russo-Persian_War_(1804%E2%80%931813) "Russo-Persian War (1804–1813)")

Hoping to isolate and weaken Britain economically through his [Continental System](/wiki/Continental_System "Continental System"), Napoleon launched an [invasion of Portugal](/wiki/Invasion_of_Portugal_(1807) "Invasion of Portugal (1807)"), the only remaining British ally in continental Europe. After occupying [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") in November 1807, and with the bulk of French troops present in Spain, Napoleon seized the opportunity to turn against his former ally, depose the reigning [Spanish royal family](/wiki/Spanish_royal_family "Spanish royal family"), and declare his brother as [Joseph I](/wiki/Joseph_Bonaparte "Joseph Bonaparte") the King of Spain in 1808, to the disapproval of the vast majority of the Spanish populace. Spain subsequently joined Britain and Portugal, with the three powers engaging France in the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War"). The diversion of French armies to the large new Anglo-Spanish front led to Austria reentering the conflict and forming the Fifth Coalition in April 1809, principally composed of Austria, Spain, and Britain. At first, the Austrians won a significant victory [at Aspern-Essling](/wiki/Battle_of_Aspern-Essling "Battle of Aspern-Essling") but they were quickly defeated [at Wagram](/wiki/Battle_of_Wagram "Battle of Wagram"), forcing the imposition of an even harsher peace in October 1809. Britain, Spain, and Portugal remained at war with France.

Concurrently Russia, unwilling to bear the economic consequences of reduced trade, routinely violated the Continental System, prompting Napoleon to [launch a massive invasion in June 1812](/wiki/French_invasion_of_Russia "French invasion of Russia"). The resulting campaign was costly for both sides, but ultimately ended in disaster for France and the near-destruction of Napoleon's *[Grande Armée](/wiki/Grande_Arm%C3%A9e "Grande Armée")*. French forces retreated from Russian territory by December 1812 and began reconstituting their lost strength. Encouraged by the defeat, Austria, Prussia, and several minor powers joined Russia, Britain, and Spain in a Sixth Coalition and began a campaign against France. The Sixth Coalition decisively defeated Napoleon [at Leipzig](/wiki/Battle_of_Leipzig "Battle of Leipzig") in October 1813. The allies then invaded France on two fronts: the Russians, Austrians, and Prussians (plus minor allies) [invaded France from the east](/wiki/Campaign_in_north-east_France_(1814) "Campaign in north-east France (1814)"), while the British, Spanish, and Portuguese (plus minor allies) [invaded France from the west](/wiki/Campaign_in_south-west_France_(1814) "Campaign in south-west France (1814)"). Coalition troops [captured Paris](/wiki/Battle_of_Paris_(1814) "Battle of Paris (1814)") at the end of March 1814, [forced Napoleon to abdicate](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fontainebleau_(1814) "Treaty of Fontainebleau (1814)") in April, exiled him to the island of [Elba](/wiki/Principality_of_Elba "Principality of Elba"), and [restored power to the Bourbons](/wiki/First_Restoration "First Restoration").

Napoleon escaped from exile in February 1815 and reassumed control of France for around one hundred days, igniting [the eponymous conflict](/wiki/Hundred_Days "Hundred Days"). The allies formed the Seventh Coalition, which defeated him at [Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") in June 1815 and exiled him to the island of [Saint Helena](/wiki/Saint_Helena "Saint Helena"), where he died six years later in 1821.[[37]](#cite_note-zamoy-57)

The wars had profound consequences on global history. Radically new methods of warfare were introduced, including the use of [mass conscription](/wiki/Lev%C3%A9e_en_masse "Levée en masse") and [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare"). Politically, the wars greatly influenced Europe through the advancements in [civil law](/wiki/Napoleonic_Code#Codes_in_other_countries "Napoleonic Code") brought about by the [Napoleonic Code](/wiki/Napoleonic_Code "Napoleonic Code"), which was largely retained where it was adopted, and the spread of [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") and [liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism"). [Independence movements](/wiki/Spanish_American_wars_of_independence "Spanish American wars of independence") emerged in [Spanish America](/wiki/Spanish_America "Spanish America") as a direct consequence of the wars, leading to the decline of the [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire") and [Portuguese Empires](/wiki/Portuguese_Empire "Portuguese Empire"). France's defeat led to the rise of Britain as the world's [foremost naval and economic power](/wiki/Pax_Britannica "Pax Britannica"), a status it would hold for the remainder of the century. After the end of the Napoleonic Wars, the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") redrew Europe's borders and brought [a relative peace](/wiki/Concert_of_Europe "Concert of Europe") to the continent, with no major [great power](/wiki/Great_power "Great power") conflicts until the [Crimean War](/wiki/Crimean_War "Crimean War") in 1853.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

See also: [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Valmy_Battle_painting.jpg/250px-Valmy_Battle_painting.jpg)](/wiki/File:Valmy_Battle_painting.jpg)

French victory over the Prussians at the [Battle of Valmy](/wiki/Battle_of_Valmy "Battle of Valmy") in 1792

The outbreak of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") had been received with great alarm by the rulers of Europe's continental powers, further exacerbated by the [execution of Louis XVI](/wiki/Execution_of_Louis_XVI "Execution of Louis XVI"), and the [overthrow of the French monarchy](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"). In 1793, [Austria](/wiki/Habsburg_monarchy "Habsburg monarchy"), the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)"), the [Kingdom of Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples"), [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia"), the [Kingdom of Spain](/wiki/History_of_Spain_(1700-1808) "History of Spain (1700-1808)"), and the [Kingdom of Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain") formed the [First Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_First_Coalition "War of the First Coalition") to curtail the growing power of revolutionary France. Measures such as [mass conscription](/wiki/Lev%C3%A9e_en_masse "Levée en masse"), military reforms, and total war allowed France to defeat the coalition, despite the [concurrent civil war in France](/wiki/War_in_the_Vend%C3%A9e "War in the Vendée"). [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), then a general of the [French Revolutionary Army](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Army "French Revolutionary Army"), forced the Austrians to sign the [Treaty of Campo Formio](/wiki/Treaty_of_Campo_Formio "Treaty of Campo Formio"), leaving only Great Britain opposed to the fledgling French Republic.

A [Second Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Second_Coalition "War of the Second Coalition") was formed in 1798 by [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain"), [Austria](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"), [Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples"), the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States"), [Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal"), [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), and [Sweden](/wiki/Gustavian_era "Gustavian era"). The [French Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"), under the [Directory](/wiki/French_Directory "French Directory"), suffered from heavy levels of corruption and [internal strife](/wiki/Chouannerie "Chouannerie"). The new republic also lacked funds, no longer enjoying the services of [Lazare Carnot](/wiki/Lazare_Carnot "Lazare Carnot"), the [minister of war](/wiki/Minister_of_War_(France) "Minister of War (France)") who had guided France to its victories during the early stages of the [Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars"). [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), commander of the *[Armée d'Italie](/wiki/Army_of_Italy_(France) "Army of Italy (France)")* in the latter stages of the First Coalition, had [launched a campaign in Egypt](/wiki/French_invasion_of_Egypt_and_Syria "French invasion of Egypt and Syria"), intending to disrupt the British control of [India](/wiki/Company_rule_in_India "Company rule in India"). Pressed from all sides, the Republic suffered a string of successive defeats against revitalised enemies, who were supported by Britain's financial help.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Napoleon_at_the_Battle_of_Rivoli.jpg/250px-Napoleon_at_the_Battle_of_Rivoli.jpg)](/wiki/File:Napoleon_at_the_Battle_of_Rivoli.jpg)

*[The Battle of Rivoli](/wiki/The_Battle_of_Rivoli "The Battle of Rivoli")* by [Philippoteaux](/wiki/Henri_F%C3%A9lix_Emmanuel_Philippoteaux "Henri Félix Emmanuel Philippoteaux"). Bonaparte defeating the Austrians at the [Battle of Rivoli](/wiki/Battle_of_Rivoli "Battle of Rivoli") in 1797

Bonaparte returned to France from Egypt on 23 August 1799, his [campaign there having failed](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Nile "Battle of the Nile"). He seized control of the French government on 9 November, in [a bloodless coup d'état](/wiki/Coup_of_18_Brumaire "Coup of 18 Brumaire"), replacing the Directory with the [Consulate](/wiki/French_Consulate "French Consulate") and transforming the republic into a *de facto* dictatorship.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones1994[httpsarchiveorgdetailscambridgeillustr00jone_0page193_193–194]-58) He further reorganised the French military forces, establishing a large reserve army positioned to support campaigns on the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") or in Italy. [Russia had already been knocked out of the war](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Zurich "Second Battle of Zurich"), and, under Napoleon's leadership, the French [decisively defeated the Austrians in June 1800](/wiki/Battle_of_Marengo "Battle of Marengo"), crippling Austrian capabilities in Italy. Austria was [definitively defeated that December](/wiki/Battle_of_Hohenlinden "Battle of Hohenlinden"), by [Moreau](/wiki/Jean_Victor_Marie_Moreau "Jean Victor Marie Moreau")'s forces in [Bavaria](/wiki/Electorate_of_Bavaria "Electorate of Bavaria"). The Austrian defeat was sealed by the [Treaty of Lunéville](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lun%C3%A9ville "Treaty of Lunéville") early the following year, further compelling the British to sign the [Treaty of Amiens](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amiens "Treaty of Amiens") with France, establishing a tenuous peace.

### Start date and nomenclature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Start date and nomenclature")]

No consensus exists as to when the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") ended and the Napoleonic Wars began. Possible dates include 9 November 1799, when Bonaparte [seized power on 18 Brumaire](/wiki/Coup_of_18_Brumaire "Coup of 18 Brumaire"), the date according to the [Republican Calendar](/wiki/French_Republican_Calendar "French Republican Calendar") then in use;[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcLynn1998215-59) 18 May 1803, when Britain and France ended the one short period of peace between 1792 and 1814; or 2 December 1804, when [Bonaparte crowned himself Emperor](/wiki/Coronation_of_Napoleon "Coronation of Napoleon").[[40]](#cite_note-60)

British historians occasionally refer to the nearly continuous period of warfare from 1792 to 1815 as the Great French War, or as the final phase of the Anglo-French [Second Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Second_Hundred_Years%27_War "Second Hundred Years' War"), spanning the period 1689 to 1815.[[41]](#cite_note-61) Historian Mike Rapport (2013) suggested using the term "French Wars" to unambiguously describe the entire period from 1792 to 1815.[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERapport20134-62)

In France, the Napoleonic Wars are generally integrated with the French Revolutionary Wars: **Les guerres de la Révolution et de l'Empire**.[[43]](#cite_note-63)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

German historiography may count the War of the Second Coalition (1798/9–1801/2), during which Napoleon had seized power, as the *Erster Napoleonischer Krieg* ("First Napoleonic War").[[44]](#cite_note-64)

In Dutch historiography, it is common to refer to the 7 major wars between 1792 and 1815 as the [Coalition Wars](/wiki/Coalition_Wars "Coalition Wars") (*coalitieoorlogen*), referring to the first two as the French Revolution Wars (*Franse Revolutieoorlogen*).[[45]](#cite_note-Encarta_coalitieoorlogen-65)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Napoleon's tactics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Napoleon's tactics")]

[Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") was, and remains, famous for his battlefield victories, and historians have spent enormous attention in analysing them.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandler1966-66)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] In 2008, Donald Sutherland wrote:

> The ideal Napoleonic battle was to manipulate the enemy into an unfavourable position through manoeuvre and deception, force him to commit his main forces and reserve to the main battle and then undertake an enveloping attack with uncommitted or reserve troops on the flank or rear. Such a surprise attack would either produce a devastating effect on morale or force him to weaken his main battle line. Either way, the enemy's own impulsiveness began the process by which even a smaller French army could defeat the enemy's forces one by one.[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESutherland2008[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidDuwHSN9QTroCpgPA356_356]-67)

After 1807, Napoleon's creation of a highly mobile, well-armed artillery force gave artillery usage an increased tactical importance. Napoleon, rather than relying on infantry to wear away the enemy's defences, could now use massed artillery as a spearhead to pound a break in the enemy's line. Once that was achieved he sent in infantry and cavalry.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcConachy2001617–640-68)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

## Prelude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Prelude")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Battle_of_Zurich.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Zurich.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Zurich.jpg)

*[The Battle of Zurich](/wiki/The_Battle_of_Zurich "The Battle of Zurich")* by [François Bouchot](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Bouchot "François Bouchot"). French victory over the Austrians and Russians at the [Second Battle of Zürich](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Zurich "Second Battle of Zurich")

Britain was irritated by several French actions following the [Treaty of Amiens](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amiens "Treaty of Amiens"). Bonaparte annexed [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont "Piedmont") and [Elba](/wiki/Elba "Elba"), made himself President of the [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italian_Republic_(Napoleonic) "Italian Republic (Napoleonic)"), a state in northern Italy that France had set up, and failed to evacuate [Holland](/wiki/Batavian_Republic "Batavian Republic"), as it had agreed to do in the treaty. France then continued to interfere with British trade despite peace having been made and complained about Britain harbouring certain individuals and not cracking down on the anti-French press.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdams1805220–222-69)

[Malta](/wiki/Malta_Protectorate "Malta Protectorate") was captured by Britain during the war and was subject to a complex arrangement in the 10th article of the Treaty of Amiens, where it was to be restored to the [Knights of St. John](/wiki/Knights_of_St._John "Knights of St. John") with a [Neapolitan](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples") garrison and placed under the guarantee of third powers. The weakening of the Knights of St. John by the confiscation of their assets in France and Spain along with delays in obtaining guarantees prevented the British from evacuating it after three months as stipulated in the treaty.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdams1805239-70)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Philip_James_de_Loutherbourg_-_The_Battle_of_Alexandria%2C_21_March_1801_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Philip_James_de_Loutherbourg_-_The_Battle_of_Alexandria%2C_21_March_1801_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Philip_James_de_Loutherbourg_-_The_Battle_of_Alexandria,_21_March_1801_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*[The Battle of Alexandria](/wiki/The_Battle_of_Alexandria_(painting) "The Battle of Alexandria (painting)")*. The British victory over the French at the [Battle of Alexandria](/wiki/Battle_of_Alexandria_(1801) "Battle of Alexandria (1801)"), resulted in the end of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s military presence in Egypt.

The [Helvetic Republic](/wiki/Helvetic_Republic "Helvetic Republic") was set up by France when it [invaded Switzerland in 1798](/wiki/French_invasion_of_Switzerland "French invasion of Switzerland"). France had withdrawn its troops, but [violent strife broke out against the government](/wiki/Stecklikrieg "Stecklikrieg"), which many Swiss saw as overly centralised. Bonaparte reoccupied the country in October 1802 and [imposed a compromise settlement](/wiki/Act_of_Mediation "Act of Mediation"). This caused widespread outrage in Britain, which protested that this was a violation of the [Treaty of Lunéville](/wiki/Treaty_of_Lun%C3%A9ville "Treaty of Lunéville"). Although continental powers were unprepared to act, the British decided to send an agent to help the Swiss obtain supplies, and also sent secret orders to the [Cape Colony](/wiki/Dutch_Cape_Colony "Dutch Cape Colony") to prevent a Dutch takeover.[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdams1805248–252-71)

Swiss resistance collapsed before anything could be accomplished, and, after a month, Britain countermanded the orders to not restore Cape Colony. At the same time, Russia finally joined the guarantee regarding Malta. Concerned that there would be hostilities when Bonaparte found out that Cape Colony had been [retained](/wiki/Battle_of_Muizenberg "Battle of Muizenberg"), the British began to procrastinate on the evacuation of Malta.[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdams1805252–258-72) In January 1803, a government paper in France published a report from a commercial agent which noted the ease with which [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_Eyalet "Egypt Eyalet") could be conquered. The British seized on this to demand satisfaction and security before evacuating Malta, which was a convenient stepping stone to Egypt. France disclaimed any desire to seize Egypt and asked what sort of satisfaction was required, but the British were unable to give a response.[[53]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdams1805258–264-73) There was still no thought of going to war; Prime Minister [Henry Addington](/wiki/Henry_Addington "Henry Addington") publicly affirmed that Britain was in a state of peace.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdams1805265-74)

In early March 1803, the [Addington ministry](/wiki/Addington_ministry "Addington ministry") received word that Cape Colony had been reoccupied by the British army, in accordance with the orders which had subsequently been countermanded. On 8 March they ordered military preparations to guard against possible French retaliation and justified them by claiming that it was only in response to French preparations and that they were conducting serious negotiations with France. In a few days, it was known that Cape Colony had been handed over in accordance with the counter-orders, but it was too late. Bonaparte berated the British ambassador [Lord Whitworth](/wiki/Lord_Whitworth "Lord Whitworth") in front of 200 spectators over the military preparations.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdams1805264–268-75)

The Addington ministry realised they would face an inquiry over their reasons for the military preparations, and during April unsuccessfully attempted to secure the support of [William Pitt](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger") to shield them from damage.[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdams1805277-76) In the same month, the ministry issued an ultimatum to France, demanding a retention of [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta") for at least ten years, the permanent acquisition of the island of [Lampedusa](/wiki/Lampedusa "Lampedusa") from the [Kingdom of Sicily](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sicily "Kingdom of Sicily"), and the evacuation of [Holland](/wiki/Batavian_Republic "Batavian Republic"). They also offered to recognise French gains in Italy if they evacuated Switzerland and compensated the [Kingdom of Sardinia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Sardinia_(1720%E2%80%931861) "Kingdom of Sardinia (1720–1861)") for his territorial losses. France offered to place Malta in the hands of Russia to satisfy British concerns, pull out of Holland when Malta was evacuated, and form a convention to give satisfaction to Britain on other issues. The Addington ministry denied that Russia had made an offer, and Whitworth left [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris").[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAdams1805268–278-77) Bonaparte sent a secret offer where he agreed to let Britain retain Malta if France could occupy the [Otranto](/wiki/Otranto "Otranto") peninsula in [Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples "Kingdom of Naples").[[58]](#cite_note-78) Such efforts were futile, and Britain declared war on 18 May 1803.

## War between Britain and France, 1803–1814

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: War between Britain and France, 1803–1814")]

Main article: [United Kingdom in the Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/United_Kingdom_in_the_Napoleonic_Wars "United Kingdom in the Napoleonic Wars")

### British motivations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: British motivations")]

Britain ended the uneasy truce created by the Treaty of Amiens when it had declared war on France in May 1803. The British were increasingly angered by Napoleon's reordering of the international system in Western Europe, especially in Switzerland, Germany, Italy and the Netherlands. Kagan argues that Britain was especially alarmed by Napoleon's assertion of control over Switzerland. The British felt insulted when Napoleon said they deserved no voice in European affairs (even though King George was an [elector](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector") of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire")) and sought to censor the London newspapers that were vilifying him.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKagan200742–43-79)

Britain had a sense of loss of control, as well as loss of markets, and was worried by Napoleon's possible threat to its [overseas colonies](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"). McLynn argues that Britain went to war in 1803 out of a "mixture of economic motives and national neuroses—an irrational anxiety about Napoleon's motives and intentions." McLynn concludes that it proved to be the right choice for Britain because, in the long run, Napoleon's intentions were hostile to the British national interest. Napoleon was not ready for war, and so this was the best time for Britain to stop them. Britain seized upon the Malta issue, refusing to evacuate the island.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERoberts2014309-80)

The deeper British grievance with Napoleon was his attempts to assume personal control of Europe, making the international system unstable, and forcing Britain to the sidelines.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrainger2004Schroeder1994231-81)[[62]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBryant1944-82)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKagan200742–43-79)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Numerous scholars have argued that Napoleon's aggressive posture made him enemies and cost him potential allies.[[63]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETulard1984351-83) As late as 1808, the continental powers affirmed most of his gains and titles, but the continuing conflict with Britain led him to start the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War") and the [invasion of Russia](/wiki/French_invasion_of_Russia "French invasion of Russia"), which many scholars see as a dramatic miscalculation.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGray200747Malia2006205-84)[[65]](#cite_note-85)[[66]](#cite_note-86)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Duckworth%27s_Action_off_San_Domingo%2C_6_February_1806_RMG_BHC0571.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Duckworth%27s_Action_off_San_Domingo%2C_6_February_1806_RMG_BHC0571.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Duckworth%27s_Action_off_San_Domingo,_6_February_1806_RMG_BHC0571.tiff)

The [Battle of San Domingo](/wiki/Battle_of_San_Domingo "Battle of San Domingo"), 6 February 1806

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Battle_of_the_Pyrenees%2C_July_28th_1813_-_Fonds_Ancely_-_B315556101_A_HEATH_035_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Battle_of_the_Pyrenees%2C_July_28th_1813_-_Fonds_Ancely_-_B315556101_A_HEATH_035_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_the_Pyrenees,_July_28th_1813_-_Fonds_Ancely_-_B315556101_A_HEATH_035_(cropped).jpg)

The [Battle of the Pyrenees](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Pyrenees "Battle of the Pyrenees"), July 1813

There was one serious attempt to negotiate peace with France during the war, made by [Charles James Fox](/wiki/Charles_James_Fox "Charles James Fox") in 1806. The British offered to accept French conquests on the continent in exchange for France recognising British overseas conquests and restoring Hanover to George III. The French were willing to recognise Britain's control over Malta, the Cape Colony, [Tobago](/wiki/Tobago "Tobago"), and [French India](/wiki/French_India "French India") but wanted to obtain Sicily in exchange for the restoration of Hanover, a condition which Fox's delegation refused.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurke1808-87)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Unlike its many coalition partners, Britain remained at war during the period of the Napoleonic Wars. Protected by naval supremacy (in the alleged words of Admiral [Jervis](/wiki/John_Jervis,_1st_Earl_of_St_Vincent "John Jervis, 1st Earl of St Vincent") to the House of Lords "I do not say, my Lords, that the French will not come. I say only they will not come by sea"), Britain did not have to spend the entire war defending itself and could thus focus on supporting its embattled allies, maintaining low-intensity land warfare on a global scale for over a decade. The British government paid out a large amount of money to other European states so that they could pay armies in the field against France. These payments are colloquially known as the [Golden Cavalry of St George](/wiki/Golden_Cavalry_of_St_George "Golden Cavalry of St George"). The [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") provided long-term support to the Spanish rebellion in the Peninsular War of 1808–1814, assisted by Spanish [guerrilla](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") ('little war') tactics. Anglo-Portuguese forces under [Arthur Wellesley](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington") supported the Spanish, who campaigned successfully against the French armies, eventually driving them from Spain and allowing Britain to invade southern France. By 1815, the British Army played the central role in the final defeat of Napoleon at Waterloo.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/BritishEmpire1815.png/330px-BritishEmpire1815.png)](/wiki/File:BritishEmpire1815.png)

The British managed to occupy and take control of [Cape Colony](/wiki/Cape_Colony "Cape Colony"), [British Guiana](/wiki/British_Guiana "British Guiana"), [Malta](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Malta "Crown Colony of Malta"), [Mauritius](/wiki/British_Mauritius "British Mauritius") and [Ceylon](/wiki/British_Ceylon "British Ceylon") during the Napoleonic Wars.

Beyond minor actions against French imperial interests, the Napoleonic Wars were much less global in their scope than preceding conflicts such as the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War"), which historians term a "[world war](/wiki/World_war "World war")".

### Economic warfare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Economic warfare")]

In response to the naval blockade of the French coasts enacted by the British government on 16 May 1806, Napoleon issued the [Berlin Decree](/wiki/Berlin_Decree "Berlin Decree") on 21 November 1806, which brought into effect the [Continental System](/wiki/Continental_System "Continental System").[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchroeder1994307–310-88) This policy aimed to eliminate the threat from Britain by closing French-controlled territory to its trade. Britain maintained a standing army of 220,000 at the height of the Napoleonic Wars, of whom less than 50% were available for campaigning. The rest were necessary for garrisoning [Ireland](/wiki/British_rule_in_Ireland "British rule in Ireland") and the colonies and providing security for Britain. France's strength peaked at around 2,500,000 full-time and part-time soldiers including several hundred thousand [National Guardsmen](/wiki/National_Guard_(France) "National Guard (France)") whom Napoleon could draft into the military if necessary. Both nations enlisted large numbers of sedentary militia who were unsuited for campaigning and were mostly employed to release regular forces for active duty.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy1989128–129-89)

The Royal Navy disrupted France's extra-continental trade by seizing and threatening French shipping and [colonial possessions](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire"), but could do nothing about France's trade with the major continental economies, and posed little threat to French territory in Europe. France's population and agricultural capacity greatly outstripped Britain's. Britain had the greatest industrial capacity in Europe, and its mastery of the seas allowed it to build up considerable economic strength through trade. This ensured that France could never consolidate its control over Europe in peace. Many in the French government believed that cutting Britain off from the Continent would end its economic influence over Europe and isolate it.

### Financing the war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Financing the war")]

A key element in British success was its ability to mobilise the nation's industrial and financial resources, and apply them to defeating France. Though the UK had a population of approximately 16 million against France's 30 million, the French numerical advantage was offset by British subsidies that paid for many of the Austrian and Russian soldiers, peaking at about 450,000 men in 1813.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy1989128–129-89)[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESherwig1969-90)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Under the Anglo–Russian agreement of 1803, Britain paid a subsidy of £1.5 million for every 100,000 Russian soldiers in the field.[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer197486-91)

British national output continued to be strong, and the well-organised business sector channeled products into what the military needed. Britain used its economic power to expand the Royal Navy, doubling the number of [frigates](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate"), adding 50 per cent more large [ships of the line](/wiki/Ship_of_the_line "Ship of the line"), and increasing the number of sailors from 15,000 to 133,000 in eight years after the war began in 1793. France saw its navy shrink by more than half.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBriggs1959143-92) The smuggling of finished products into the continent undermined French efforts to weaken the British economy by cutting off markets. Subsidies to Russia and Austria kept them in the war. The British budget in 1814 reached £98 million, including £10 million for the Royal Navy, £40 million for the army, £10 million for the allies, and £38 million as interest on the national debt, which had soared to £679 million, more than double the GDP. This debt was supported by hundreds of thousands of investors and taxpayers, despite the higher taxes on land and a new income tax. The cost of the war amounted to £831 million.[[u]](#cite_note-94) In contrast, the French financial system was inadequate and Napoleon's forces had to rely in part on requisitions from conquered lands.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHalévy1924205–228-95)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKnight2013-96)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[76]](#cite_note-97)

From London in 1813 to 1815, [Nathan Mayer Rothschild](/wiki/Nathan_Mayer_Rothschild "Nathan Mayer Rothschild") was crucial in almost single-handedly financing the British war effort, organising the shipment of bullion to the Duke of Wellington's armies across Europe, as well as arranging the payment of British financial subsidies to their continental allies.[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFerguson200878-98)

## War of the Third Coalition, 1805

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: War of the Third Coalition, 1805")]

Main article: [War of the Third Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Third_Coalition "War of the Third Coalition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Trafalgar-Auguste_Mayer.jpg/250px-Trafalgar-Auguste_Mayer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trafalgar-Auguste_Mayer.jpg)

The British [HMS *Sandwich*](/wiki/HMS_Sandwich_(1759) "HMS Sandwich (1759)") fires at the French [flagship](/wiki/Flagship "Flagship") *[Bucentaure](/wiki/French_ship_Bucentaure_(1803) "French ship Bucentaure (1803)")* (completely dismasted) in the [battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar"). *Bucentaure* also fights [HMS *Victory*](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory") (behind her) and [HMS *Temeraire*](/wiki/HMS_Temeraire_(1798) "HMS Temeraire (1798)") (left side of the picture). HMS *Sandwich* did not fight at Trafalgar and her depiction is a mistake by the painter.[[78]](#cite_note-99)

Britain gathered allies to form the Third Coalition against The French Empire after Napoleon was self-proclaimed as emperor.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchroeder1994231–86-100)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[80]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKagan2007141ff-101) In response, Napoleon [seriously considered an invasion of Great Britain](/wiki/Napoleon%27s_planned_invasion_of_the_United_Kingdom "Napoleon's planned invasion of the United Kingdom"),[[81]](#cite_note-102)[[82]](#cite_note-103) massing 180,000 troops at [Boulogne](/wiki/Boulogne-sur-Mer "Boulogne-sur-Mer"). Before he could invade, he needed to achieve naval superiority—or at least to pull the British fleet away from the [English Channel](/wiki/English_Channel "English Channel"). A complex plan to distract the British by threatening their possessions in the [West Indies](/wiki/West_Indies "West Indies") failed when a Franco-Spanish fleet under Admiral [Villeneuve](/wiki/Pierre-Charles_de_Villeneuve "Pierre-Charles de Villeneuve") turned back after an indecisive action off [Cape Finisterre](/wiki/Battle_of_Cape_Finisterre_(1805) "Battle of Cape Finisterre (1805)") on 22 July 1805. The Royal Navy blockaded Villeneuve in [Cádiz](/wiki/C%C3%A1diz "Cádiz") until he left for [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") on 19 October; the British squadron caught and overwhelmingly defeated a Franco-Spanish fleet in the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar") on 21 October (the British commander, [Lord Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson,_1st_Viscount_Nelson "Horatio Nelson, 1st Viscount Nelson"), died in the battle). Napoleon never again had the opportunity to challenge the British at sea, nor to threaten an invasion. He again turned his attention to the enemies on the Continent.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Strategic_Situation_of_Europe_1805.jpg/330px-Strategic_Situation_of_Europe_1805.jpg)](/wiki/File:Strategic_Situation_of_Europe_1805.jpg)

European strategic situation in 1805 before the War of the Third Coalition

In April 1805, Britain and Russia signed a treaty with the aim of removing the French from the [Batavian Republic](/wiki/Batavian_Republic "Batavian Republic") (roughly present-day Netherlands) and the [Swiss Confederation](/wiki/Swiss_Confederation_(Napoleonic) "Swiss Confederation (Napoleonic)"). Austria joined the alliance after the annexation of [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa") ([Ligurian Republic](/wiki/Ligurian_Republic "Ligurian Republic")) and the proclamation of Napoleon as [King of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Italy (Napoleonic)") on 17 March 1805. Sweden, which had already agreed to lease [Swedish Pomerania](/wiki/Swedish_Pomerania "Swedish Pomerania") as a military base for British troops against France, entered the coalition on 9 August.

The Austrians began the war by invading [Bavaria](/wiki/Electorate_of_Bavaria "Electorate of Bavaria") on 8 September 1805 with an army of about 72,000 under [Karl Mack von Leiberich](/wiki/Karl_Mack_von_Leiberich "Karl Mack von Leiberich"),[[83]](#cite_note-104) and the French army marched out from [Boulogne](/wiki/Camp_of_Boulogne "Camp of Boulogne") in late July 1805 to confront them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] At [Ulm](/wiki/Battle_of_Ulm "Battle of Ulm") (25 September – 20 October) Napoleon surrounded Mack's army, forcing its surrender without significant losses.

With the main Austrian army north of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") defeated (another army under [Archduke Charles](/wiki/Archduke_Charles_of_Austria "Archduke Charles of Austria") fought against [André Masséna](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Mass%C3%A9na "André Masséna")'s [French army in Italy](/wiki/Army_of_Italy_(France) "Army of Italy (France)")), Napoleon occupied [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") on 13 November. Far from his supply lines, he faced a larger Austro–Russian army under the command of [Mikhail Kutuzov](/wiki/Mikhail_Kutuzov "Mikhail Kutuzov"), with Emperor [Alexander I of Russia](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Russia "Alexander I of Russia") personally present. On 2 December, Napoleon crushed the Austro–Russian force in [Moravia](/wiki/Moravia "Moravia") at [Austerlitz](/wiki/Battle_of_Austerlitz "Battle of Austerlitz") (usually considered his greatest victory). He inflicted 25,000 casualties on a numerically superior enemy army while sustaining fewer than 7,000 in his own force.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Charles_Th%C3%A9venin_-_Reddition_de_la_ville_d%27Ulm.jpg/250px-Charles_Th%C3%A9venin_-_Reddition_de_la_ville_d%27Ulm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Th%C3%A9venin_-_Reddition_de_la_ville_d%27Ulm.jpg)

Surrender of the town of [Ulm](/wiki/Battle_of_Ulm "Battle of Ulm"), 20 October 1805

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Entr%C3%A9e_des_Francaise_dans_Vienne_le_13_Novembre_1805.jpg/250px-Entr%C3%A9e_des_Francaise_dans_Vienne_le_13_Novembre_1805.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entr%C3%A9e_des_Francaise_dans_Vienne_le_13_Novembre_1805.jpg)

The French entering [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") on 13 November 1805

Austria signed the [Treaty of Pressburg](/wiki/Treaty_of_Pressburg_(1805) "Treaty of Pressburg (1805)") (26 December 1805) and left the coalition. The treaty required the Austrians to give up [Venetia](/wiki/Venetia_(region) "Venetia (region)") to the French-dominated [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Italy (Napoleonic)") and the [Tyrol](/wiki/German_Tyrol "German Tyrol") to Bavaria. With the withdrawal of Austria from the war, stalemate ensued. Napoleon's army had a record of continuous unbroken victories on land, but the full force of the Russian army had not yet come into play. Napoleon had now consolidated his hold on France, had taken control of Belgium, the Netherlands, Switzerland, and most of Western Germany and northern Italy. His admirers say that Napoleon wanted to stop now, but was forced to continue in order to gain greater security from the countries that refused to accept his conquests. Esdaile rejects that explanation and instead says that it was a good time to stop expansion, for the major powers were ready to accept Napoleon as he was:

> in 1806 both Russia and Britain had been positively eager to make peace, and they might well have agreed to terms that would have left the Napoleonic imperium almost completely intact. As for Austria and Prussia, they simply wanted to be left alone. To have secured a compromise peace, then, would have been comparatively easy. But Napoleon was prepared to make no concessions.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEsdaile2009252–253-105)

## War of the Fourth Coalition, 1806–1807

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: War of the Fourth Coalition, 1806–1807")]

Main article: [War of the Fourth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Fourth_Coalition "War of the Fourth Coalition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Charles_Meynier_-_Entr%C3%A9e_de_Napol%C3%A9on_%C3%A0_Berlin._27_octobre_1806.jpg/250px-Charles_Meynier_-_Entr%C3%A9e_de_Napol%C3%A9on_%C3%A0_Berlin._27_octobre_1806.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charles_Meynier_-_Entr%C3%A9e_de_Napol%C3%A9on_%C3%A0_Berlin._27_octobre_1806.jpg)

*[Entry of Napoleon into Berlin](/wiki/Entry_of_Napoleon_into_Berlin "Entry of Napoleon into Berlin")*. After defeating Prussian forces at [Jena](/wiki/Battle_of_Jena-Auerstedt "Battle of Jena-Auerstedt"), the French Army entered Berlin on 27 October 1806.

Within months of the collapse of the Third Coalition, the [Fourth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Fourth_Coalition "War of the Fourth Coalition") (1806–1807) against France was formed by Britain, Prussia, Russia, [Saxony](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Saxony "Kingdom of Saxony"), and Sweden. In July 1806, Napoleon formed the [Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Confederation of the Rhine") out of the many small German states which constituted the [Rhineland](/wiki/Rhineland "Rhineland") and most other western parts of Germany. He [amalgamated many of the smaller states](/wiki/German_mediatisation "German mediatisation") into larger electorates, duchies, and kingdoms to make the governance of non-Prussian Germany smoother. Napoleon elevated the rulers of the two largest Confederation states, [Saxony](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Saxony "Kingdom of Saxony") and [Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria"), to the status of kings.

In August 1806, the Prussian king, [Frederick William III](/wiki/Frederick_William_III_of_Prussia "Frederick William III of Prussia"), decided to go to war independently of any other great power. The army of Russia, a Prussian ally, in particular, was too far away to assist. On 8 October 1806, Napoleon unleashed all the French forces east of the Rhine into Prussia. Napoleon defeated a Prussian army at [Jena](/wiki/Battle_of_Jena "Battle of Jena") (14 October 1806), and [Davout](/wiki/Louis-Nicolas_Davout "Louis-Nicolas Davout") defeated another at [Auerstädt](/wiki/Battle_of_Auerst%C3%A4dt "Battle of Auerstädt") on the same day. 160,000 French soldiers (increasing in number as the campaign went on) attacked Prussia, moving with such speed that they destroyed the entire [Prussian Army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army") as an effective military force. Out of 250,000 troops, the Prussians sustained 25,000 casualties, lost a further 150,000 as prisoners, 4,000 artillery pieces, and over 100,000 muskets. At Jena, Napoleon had fought only a detachment of the Prussian force. The battle at Auerstädt involved a single French corps defeating the bulk of the Prussian army. Napoleon entered Berlin on 27 October 1806. He visited the tomb of [Frederick the Great](/wiki/Frederick_the_Great "Frederick the Great") and instructed his [marshals](/wiki/Marshal_of_the_Empire "Marshal of the Empire") to remove their hats there, saying, "If he were alive we wouldn't be here today". Napoleon had taken only 19 days from beginning his attack on Prussia to knock it out of the war with the capture of Berlin and the destruction of its principal armies at Jena and Auerstädt. Saxony abandoned Prussia, and together with small states from north Germany, allied with France.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Friedland_mazurovsky.jpg/250px-Friedland_mazurovsky.jpg)](/wiki/File:Friedland_mazurovsky.jpg)

Charge of the Russian [Imperial Guard](/wiki/Imperial_Guard_(Russia) "Imperial Guard (Russia)") cavalry against French cuirassiers at the [Battle of Friedland](/wiki/Battle_of_Friedland "Battle of Friedland"), 14 June 1807

In the next stage of the war, the French drove Russian forces out of Poland and employed many Polish and German soldiers in several sieges in [Silesia](/wiki/Silesia "Silesia") and [Pomerania](/wiki/Pomerania "Pomerania"), with the assistance of Dutch and Italian soldiers in the latter case. Napoleon then turned north to confront the remainder of the Russian army and to try to capture the temporary Prussian capital at [Königsberg](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigsberg "Königsberg"). A tactical draw at [Eylau](/wiki/Battle_of_Eylau "Battle of Eylau") (7–8 February 1807), followed by capitulation at [Danzig](/wiki/Siege_of_Danzig_(1807) "Siege of Danzig (1807)") (24 May 1807) and the [Battle of Heilsberg](/wiki/Battle_of_Heilsberg "Battle of Heilsberg") (10 June 1807), forced the Russians to withdraw further north. Napoleon decisively beat the Russian army at [Friedland](/wiki/Battle_of_Friedland "Battle of Friedland") (14 June 1807), following which Alexander had to make peace with Napoleon at [Tilsit](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tilsit "Treaty of Tilsit") (7 July 1807). In Germany and Poland, new Napoleonic client states, such as the [Kingdom of Westphalia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Westphalia "Kingdom of Westphalia"), [Duchy of Warsaw](/wiki/Duchy_of_Warsaw "Duchy of Warsaw"), and [Republic of Danzig](/wiki/Free_City_of_Danzig_(Napoleonic) "Free City of Danzig (Napoleonic)"), were established. Early that same year, the French [besieged the fortified town of Kolberg](/wiki/Siege_of_Kolberg_(1807) "Siege of Kolberg (1807)"), which resulted in a lifting of the siege by a peace treaty on 2 July.

By September, Marshal [Guillaume Brune](/wiki/Guillaume_Brune "Guillaume Brune") completed the occupation of [Swedish Pomerania](/wiki/Swedish_Pomerania "Swedish Pomerania"), allowing the Swedish army to withdraw with all its munitions of war.

### Scandinavia and Finland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Scandinavia and Finland")]

Main articles: [Gunboat War](/wiki/Gunboat_War "Gunboat War"), [Finnish War](/wiki/Finnish_War "Finnish War"), and [Dano-Swedish War of 1808–1809](/wiki/Dano-Swedish_War_of_1808%E2%80%931809 "Dano-Swedish War of 1808–1809")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Battle_of_Trangen_%28cropped%29.JPG/250px-Battle_of_Trangen_%28cropped%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Trangen_(cropped).JPG)

The [Battle of Trangen](/wiki/Battle_of_Trangen "Battle of Trangen") during the [Dano–Swedish War, 1808–1809](/wiki/Dano-Swedish_War_of_1808%E2%80%931809 "Dano-Swedish War of 1808–1809").

Britain's first response to Napoleon's Continental System was to launch a major naval attack against [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark-Norway "Denmark-Norway"). Although ostensibly neutral, Denmark was under heavy French and Russian pressure to pledge [its fleet](/wiki/Royal_Danish_Navy "Royal Danish Navy") to Napoleon. London could not take the chance of ignoring the Danish threat. In August 1807, the Royal Navy [besieged and bombarded Copenhagen](/wiki/Battle_of_Copenhagen_(1807) "Battle of Copenhagen (1807)"), leading to the capture of the [Dano–Norwegian fleet](/wiki/History_of_the_Danish_navy "History of the Danish navy"), and assuring use of the sea lanes in the North and Baltic seas for the British merchant fleet. Denmark joined the war on the side of France, but without a fleet it had little to offer,[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERyan1953-106)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*][[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMunch-Petersen2007-107)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] beginning an engagement in a [naval guerrilla war](/wiki/Gunboat_War "Gunboat War") in which small gunboats attacked larger British ships in Danish and Norwegian waters. Denmark also committed themselves to participate in a war against Sweden together with France and Russia.

At Tilsit, Napoleon and Alexander had agreed that Russia should force Sweden to join the Continental System, which led to a [Russian invasion of Finland](/wiki/Finnish_War "Finnish War") in February 1808, followed by a [Danish declaration of war](/wiki/Dano-Swedish_War_of_1808%E2%80%931809 "Dano-Swedish War of 1808–1809") in March. Napoleon also sent an auxiliary corps, consisting of troops from France, [Spain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Spain_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Spain (Napoleonic)") and [Holland](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Holland "Kingdom of Holland"), led by Marshal [Jean-Baptiste Bernadotte](/wiki/Jean-Baptiste_Bernadotte "Jean-Baptiste Bernadotte"), to Denmark to participate in the invasion of Sweden. But British naval superiority prevented the armies from crossing the [Øresund](/wiki/%C3%98resund "Øresund") strait, and the war came mainly to be fought along the Swedish–Norwegian border. At the [Congress of Erfurt](/wiki/Congress_of_Erfurt "Congress of Erfurt") (September–October 1808), France and Russia further agreed on the division of Sweden into two parts separated by the [Gulf of Bothnia](/wiki/Gulf_of_Bothnia "Gulf of Bothnia"), where the eastern part became the Russian [Grand Duchy of Finland](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Finland "Grand Duchy of Finland"). British voluntary attempts to assist Sweden with humanitarian aid remained limited, and did not prevent Sweden from adopting a more Napoleon-friendly policy.[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGötz2014519–539-108)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

The war between Denmark and Britain effectively finished with a British victory at the [Battle of Lyngør](/wiki/Battle_of_Lyng%C3%B8r "Battle of Lyngør") in 1812, involving the destruction of the last large Dano–Norwegian ship—the [frigate](/wiki/Frigate "Frigate") *[Najaden](/wiki/HDMS_Najaden_(1811) "HDMS Najaden (1811)")*.

### Poland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Poland")]

Main article: [Duchy of Warsaw](/wiki/Duchy_of_Warsaw "Duchy of Warsaw")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/January_Suchodolski%2C_Bitwa_pod_Somosierr%C4%85.jpg/250px-January_Suchodolski%2C_Bitwa_pod_Somosierr%C4%85.jpg)](/wiki/File:January_Suchodolski,_Bitwa_pod_Somosierr%C4%85.jpg)

[Polish cavalry](/wiki/Polish_cavalry "Polish cavalry") at the [Battle of Somosierra](/wiki/Battle_of_Somosierra "Battle of Somosierra") in Spain, 1808

In 1807, Napoleon created a powerful outpost of his empire in Central Europe. [Poland](/wiki/History_of_Poland "History of Poland") had recently been [partitioned by its three neighbours](/wiki/Partitions_of_Poland "Partitions of Poland"), but Napoleon created the Grand Duchy of Warsaw, which depended on France from the beginning. The duchy consisted of lands seized by Austria and Prussia; its Grand Duke was Napoleon's ally King [Frederick Augustus I of Saxony](/wiki/Frederick_Augustus_I_of_Saxony "Frederick Augustus I of Saxony"), but Napoleon appointed the intendants who administered the country. The population of 4.3 million was released from occupation and, by 1814, sent about 200,000 men to Napoleon's armies. That included about 90,000 who marched with him to Moscow; few marched back.[[88]](#cite_note-109) The Russians strongly opposed any move towards an independent Poland and one reason Napoleon invaded Russia in 1812 was to punish them. The Grand Duchy was absorbed into the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") as a semi-autonomous [Congress Poland](/wiki/Congress_Poland "Congress Poland") in 1815; Poland did not become a sovereign state again until 1918, following the collapse of the neighbouring Russian, [German](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") and [Austro-Hungarian](/wiki/Austria%E2%80%93Hungary "Austria–Hungary") Empires in the aftermath of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"). Napoleon's impact on Poland was significant, including the Napoleonic legal code, the abolition of [serfdom](/wiki/Serfdom "Serfdom"), and the introduction of modern middle-class bureaucracies.[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiley201327–28-110)[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrab2003176–187-111)

## Peninsular War, 1808–1814

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Peninsular War, 1808–1814")]

Main article: [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Antoine-Jean_Gros_-_Capitulation_de_Madrid%2C_le_4_d%C3%A9cembre_1808.jpg/250px-Antoine-Jean_Gros_-_Capitulation_de_Madrid%2C_le_4_d%C3%A9cembre_1808.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antoine-Jean_Gros_-_Capitulation_de_Madrid,_le_4_d%C3%A9cembre_1808.jpg)

*[Napoleon Accepting the Surrender of Madrid](/wiki/Napoleon_Accepting_the_Surrender_of_Madrid "Napoleon Accepting the Surrender of Madrid")* by [Antoine-Jean Gros](/wiki/Antoine-Jean_Gros "Antoine-Jean Gros") (1810)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/El_dos_de_mayo_de_1808_en_Madrid.jpg/250px-El_dos_de_mayo_de_1808_en_Madrid.jpg)](/wiki/File:El_dos_de_mayo_de_1808_en_Madrid.jpg)

*[The Second of May 1808: The Charge of the Mamelukes](/wiki/The_Second_of_May_1808 "The Second of May 1808")*, by [Francisco de Goya](/wiki/Francisco_de_Goya "Francisco de Goya") (1814)

The Iberian conflict began when [Portugal](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Portugal "Kingdom of Portugal") continued trade with Britain, despite French restrictions. When [Spain](/wiki/History_of_Spain_(1700-1808) "History of Spain (1700-1808)") failed to maintain the Continental System, the uneasy Spanish alliance with France ended in all but name. French troops gradually encroached on Spanish territory until they occupied [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), and installed a client monarchy. This provoked an explosion of [popular rebellions](/wiki/Dos_de_Mayo_Uprising "Dos de Mayo Uprising") across Spain. Heavy British involvement soon followed.

After France suffered defeats in Spain, Napoleon took charge and enjoyed success, retaking Madrid, defeating the Spanish, and forcing a withdrawal of the heavily out-numbered British army from the Iberian Peninsula ([Battle of Corunna](/wiki/Battle_of_Corunna "Battle of Corunna"), 16 January 1809). But when he left, the [guerrilla war](/wiki/Guerrilla_war "Guerrilla war") against his forces in the countryside continued to tie down great numbers of troops. The outbreak of the [War of the Fifth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Fifth_Coalition "War of the Fifth Coalition") prevented Napoleon from successfully wrapping up operations against British forces by necessitating his departure for Austria, and he never returned to the Peninsular theatre. The British then sent in a fresh army under [Sir Arthur Wellesley (later the Duke of Wellington)](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington").[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFremont-Barnes2014-112)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] For a time, the British and Portuguese remained restricted to the area around [Lisbon](/wiki/Lisbon "Lisbon") (behind their impregnable [Lines of Torres Vedras](/wiki/Lines_of_Torres_Vedras "Lines of Torres Vedras")), while their Spanish allies were [besieged in Cádiz](/wiki/Siege_of_C%C3%A1diz "Siege of Cádiz").

The Peninsular war proved a major disaster for France. Napoleon did well when he was in direct charge, but severe losses followed his departure, as he severely underestimated how much manpower would be needed. The effort in Spain was a drain on money, manpower and prestige. Historian David Gates called it the "Spanish ulcer".[[92]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGates1986-113)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Napoleon realised it had been a disaster for his cause, writing later, "That unfortunate war destroyed me ... All the circumstances of my disasters are bound up in that fatal knot."[[93]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTETone2010[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidE2WtMq4LF90CpgPA243_243]-114)

The Peninsular campaigns witnessed 60 major battles and 30 major sieges, more than any other of the Napoleonic conflicts, and lasted over six years, far longer than any of the others. France and her allies lost at least 91,000 killed in action and 237,000 wounded in the peninsula.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClodfelter2017157-115) From 1812, the Peninsular War merged with the [War of the Sixth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Sixth_Coalition "War of the Sixth Coalition").

Main article: [War of the Fifth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Fifth_Coalition "War of the Fifth Coalition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Archduke_Charles%2C_Duke_of_Teschen%2C_with_his_Staff_at_the_Battle_of_Aspern-Essling%2C_21%E2%80%9322_May_1809_%28by_Johann_Peter_Krafft%2C_1838%29.jpg/250px-Archduke_Charles%2C_Duke_of_Teschen%2C_with_his_Staff_at_the_Battle_of_Aspern-Essling%2C_21%E2%80%9322_May_1809_%28by_Johann_Peter_Krafft%2C_1838%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Archduke_Charles,_Duke_of_Teschen,_with_his_Staff_at_the_Battle_of_Aspern-Essling,_21%E2%80%9322_May_1809_(by_Johann_Peter_Krafft,_1838).jpg)

The [Archduke Charles](/wiki/Archduke_Charles "Archduke Charles") with his staff at the Battle of Aspern-Essling

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/Wagram-Napoleon_and_Bessi%C3%A8res.jpg/250px-Wagram-Napoleon_and_Bessi%C3%A8res.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wagram-Napoleon_and_Bessi%C3%A8res.jpg)

Napoleon giving orders at the Battle of Wagram

The Fifth Coalition (1809) of Britain and Austria against France formed as Britain engaged in the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War") in Spain and Portugal. The sea became a major [theatre of war](/wiki/Theater_(warfare) "Theater (warfare)") against Napoleon's allies. Austria, previously an ally of France, took the opportunity to attempt to restore its imperial territories in Germany as held prior to Austerlitz. During the time of the Fifth Coalition, the Royal Navy won a succession of victories in the French colonies. On land the major battles included [Battles of Raszyn](/wiki/Battle_of_Raszyn "Battle of Raszyn"), [Eckmuhl](/wiki/Battle_of_Eckmuhl "Battle of Eckmuhl"), [Raab](/wiki/Battle_of_Raab "Battle of Raab"), [Aspern-Essling](/wiki/Battle_of_Aspern-Essling "Battle of Aspern-Essling"), and [Wagram](/wiki/Battle_of_Wagram "Battle of Wagram").

On land, the Fifth Coalition attempted few extensive military endeavours. One, the [Walcheren Expedition](/wiki/Walcheren_Expedition "Walcheren Expedition") of 1809, involved a dual effort by the British Army and the Royal Navy to relieve Austrian forces under intense French pressure. It ended in disaster after the Army commander, [John Pitt, 2nd Earl of Chatham](/wiki/John_Pitt,_2nd_Earl_of_Chatham "John Pitt, 2nd Earl of Chatham"), failed to capture the objective, the naval base of French-controlled [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"). For the most part of the years of the Fifth Coalition, British military operations on land (apart from the Iberian Peninsula) remained restricted to hit-and-run operations executed by the Royal Navy, which dominated the sea after having beaten down almost all substantial naval opposition from France and its allies and blockading what remained of France's naval forces in heavily fortified French-controlled ports. These rapid-attack operations were aimed mostly at destroying blockaded French naval and mercantile shipping and the disruption of French supplies, communications, and military units stationed near the coasts. Often, when British allies attempted military actions within several dozen miles or so of the sea, the Royal Navy would arrive, land troops and supplies, and aid the coalition's land forces in a concerted operation. Royal Navy ships even provided artillery support against French units when fighting strayed near enough to the coastline. The ability and quality of the land forces governed these operations. For example, when operating with inexperienced guerrilla forces in Spain, the Royal Navy sometimes failed to achieve its objectives because of the lack of manpower that the Navy's guerrilla allies had promised to supply.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Strategic_Situation_of_Europe_1809.jpg/500px-Strategic_Situation_of_Europe_1809.jpg)](/wiki/File:Strategic_Situation_of_Europe_1809.jpg)

The strategic situation in Europe in February 1809

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Europe_1812_map_en.png/500px-Europe_1812_map_en.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_1812_map_en.png)

The French Empire in 1812 at its greatest extent

Austria achieved some initial victories against the thinly spread army of Marshal [Berthier](/wiki/Louis_Alexandre_Berthier "Louis Alexandre Berthier"). Napoleon left Berthier with only 170,000 men to defend France's entire eastern frontier (in the 1790s, 800,000 men had carried out the same task, but holding a much shorter front).

In the east, the Austrians drove into the [Duchy of Warsaw](/wiki/Duchy_of_Warsaw "Duchy of Warsaw") but did not achieve their objectives at the [Battle of Raszyn](/wiki/Battle_of_Raszyn "Battle of Raszyn") on 19 April 1809. The [Army of the Duchy of Warsaw](/wiki/Army_of_the_Duchy_of_Warsaw "Army of the Duchy of Warsaw") captured [West Galicia](/wiki/West_Galicia "West Galicia") following its earlier success. Napoleon assumed personal command and bolstered the army for a counter-attack on Austria. After a few small battles, the well-run campaign forced the Austrians to withdraw from Bavaria, and Napoleon advanced into Austria. His hurried attempt to cross the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube") resulted in the major [Battle of Aspern-Essling](/wiki/Battle_of_Aspern-Essling "Battle of Aspern-Essling") (22 May 1809) – Napoleon's first significant tactical defeat. But the Austrian commander, [Archduke Charles](/wiki/Archduke_Charles,_Duke_of_Teschen "Archduke Charles, Duke of Teschen"), failed to follow up on his indecisive victory, allowing Napoleon to prepare and seize Vienna in early July. He defeated the Austrians at [Wagram](/wiki/Battle_of_Wagram "Battle of Wagram"), on 5–6 July. (It was during the middle of that battle that Marshal [Bernadotte](/wiki/Charles_XIV_John_of_Sweden "Charles XIV John of Sweden") was stripped of his command after retreating contrary to Napoleon's orders. Shortly thereafter, Bernadotte took up the offer from Sweden to fill the vacant position of Crown Prince there. Later he actively participated in wars against his former Emperor.)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Tirol_Panorama_18.jpg/250px-Tirol_Panorama_18.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tirol_Panorama_18.jpg)

[Tyrolean Rebellion](/wiki/Tyrolean_Rebellion "Tyrolean Rebellion") against French and Bavarian troops

The War of the Fifth Coalition ended with the [Treaty of Schönbrunn](/wiki/Treaty_of_Sch%C3%B6nbrunn "Treaty of Schönbrunn") (14 October 1809). In the east, only the [Tyrolese rebels](/wiki/Tyrolean_Rebellion "Tyrolean Rebellion") led by [Andreas Hofer](/wiki/Andreas_Hofer "Andreas Hofer") continued to fight the French-Bavarian army until finally defeated in November 1809. In the west, the Peninsular War continued. Economic warfare between Britain and France continued: The British continued a naval blockade of French-controlled territory. Due to military shortages and lack of organisation in French territory, many breaches of the Continental System occurred and the French Continental System was largely ineffective and did little economic damage to Great Britain. Both sides entered further conflicts in attempts to enforce their blockade. As Napoleon realised that extensive trade was going through Spain and Russia, he invaded those two countries;[[95]](#cite_note-116) the British fought the United States in the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") (1812–1815).

In 1810, the French Empire reached its greatest extent. Napoleon married [Marie-Louise](/wiki/Marie_Louise,_Duchess_of_Parma "Marie Louise, Duchess of Parma"), an Austrian Archduchess, with the aim of ensuring a more stable alliance with Austria and of providing the Emperor with an heir (something his first wife, [Joséphine](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9phine_de_Beauharnais "Joséphine de Beauharnais"), had failed to do). As well as the French Empire, Napoleon controlled the Swiss Confederation, the [Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Confederation of the Rhine"), the Duchy of Warsaw and the [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Italy (Napoleonic)"). Territories allied with the French included:

* the kingdoms of [Denmark–Norway](/wiki/Denmark%E2%80%93Norway "Denmark–Norway")
* the [Kingdom of Spain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Spain_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Spain (Napoleonic)") (under [Joseph Bonaparte](/wiki/Joseph_Bonaparte "Joseph Bonaparte"), Napoleon's elder brother)
* the [Kingdom of Westphalia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Westphalia "Kingdom of Westphalia") ([Jérôme Bonaparte](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_Bonaparte "Jérôme Bonaparte"), Napoleon's younger brother)
* the [Kingdom of Naples](/wiki/Napoleonic_Kingdom_of_Naples "Napoleonic Kingdom of Naples") (under [Joachim Murat](/wiki/Joachim_Murat "Joachim Murat"), husband of Napoleon's sister [Caroline](/wiki/Caroline_Bonaparte "Caroline Bonaparte"))
* the [Principality of Lucca and Piombino](/wiki/Principality_of_Lucca_and_Piombino "Principality of Lucca and Piombino") (under [Elisa Bonaparte](/wiki/Elisa_Bonaparte "Elisa Bonaparte") (Napoleon's sister) and her husband [Felice Baciocchi](/wiki/Felice_Pasquale_Baciocchi "Felice Pasquale Baciocchi"));

and Napoleon's former enemies, Sweden, Prussia and Austria.

## Subsidiary wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Subsidiary wars")]

The Napoleonic Wars were the direct cause of wars in the Americas and elsewhere.

### Serbian Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Serbian Revolution")]

Main article: [First Serbian Uprising](/wiki/First_Serbian_Uprising "First Serbian Uprising")

The [Serbian Revolution](/wiki/Serbian_Revolution "Serbian Revolution") coincided with the [Russo-Turkish War (1806–1812)](/wiki/Russo-Turkish_War_(1806%E2%80%931812) "Russo-Turkish War (1806–1812)") (in which the French diplomat, [Horace François Bastien Sébastiani de La Porta](/wiki/Horace_Fran%C3%A7ois_Bastien_S%C3%A9bastiani_de_La_Porta "Horace François Bastien Sébastiani de La Porta") played a very important role in provoking the war), which were a proxy conflict of the [Coalition Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_and_Napoleonic_Wars "French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars"), having most of the time [Serbs revolutionaries](/wiki/Revolutionary_Serbia "Revolutionary Serbia") the [support](/wiki/Russo-Serbian_Alliance_(1807) "Russo-Serbian Alliance (1807)") of the Russian Empire, while the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") was an [ally](/wiki/Franco-Ottoman_alliance#A_final,_but_short-lived,_alliance "Franco-Ottoman alliance") of the French Empire.[[96]](#cite_note-117)[[97]](#cite_note-Meriage-118) This was due to the fact that both empires feared [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s moves to the east as the subsequent [Peace of Pressburg](/wiki/Peace_of_Pressburg_(1805) "Peace of Pressburg (1805)") brought France into Balkan affairs.[[98]](#cite_note-:0-119) The most radical and liberal rebels were also inspired in some way by the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") (specially the rise of [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism")) and the autonomy of the [Illyrian Provinces](/wiki/Illyrian_Provinces "Illyrian Provinces") (Serbs initially felt that French presence in the region could have developed into military aid in support of the insurrection against Ottoman rule as a [sister republic](/wiki/Sister_republic "Sister republic"), but Napoleon didn't want to increase Russian or Austrian influence in the region).[[98]](#cite_note-:0-119)[[97]](#cite_note-Meriage-118)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Afanasij_Scheloumoff%2C_Battle_of_Mi%C5%A1ar.jpg/250px-Afanasij_Scheloumoff%2C_Battle_of_Mi%C5%A1ar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Afanasij_Scheloumoff,_Battle_of_Mi%C5%A1ar.jpg)

In August 1806, the rebels defeated the Ottomans at the [Battle of Mišar](/wiki/Battle_of_Mi%C5%A1ar "Battle of Mišar")

During the first phase, from 1804 to 1806, it was a [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism") [reaction](/wiki/Reactionary "Reactionary") to new abuses by the [Janissaries](/wiki/Janissaries "Janissaries") and [Dahis](/wiki/Dahis "Dahis"), after they killed [Hadji Mustafa Pasha](/wiki/Hadji_Mustafa_Pasha "Hadji Mustafa Pasha") ([vizier](/wiki/Vizier "Vizier") of the [Sanjak of Smederevo](/wiki/Sanjak_of_Smederevo "Sanjak of Smederevo")). He had created a militia of Serbs fighting the Janissaries that were expelled from [Belgrade](/wiki/Belgrade "Belgrade") and had found refuge with [Osman Pazvantoğlu](/wiki/Osman_Pazvanto%C4%9Flu "Osman Pazvantoğlu"), governor of the [Sanjak of Vidin](/wiki/Sanjak_of_Vidin "Sanjak of Vidin") (in present-day [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria")), who pursued his own policy and sought independence, which brought him into conflict with the Serbs and later with the [Sublime Porte](/wiki/Sublime_Porte "Sublime Porte"). The Janissaries managed to return to Belgrade and realized the [Slaughter of the Knezes](/wiki/Slaughter_of_the_Knezes "Slaughter of the Knezes"). Thus, the Serbs appealed to [Sultan](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan") [Selim III](/wiki/Selim_III "Selim III") for assistance against the Dahis, who had since rejected the authority of the Porte. Also, [Karađorđe](/wiki/Kara%C4%91or%C4%91e "Karađorđe") negotiated with the Austrian captain Sajtinski. At this meeting, he expressed the wish of the Serbian people that the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") receive them as a kingdom under its protection like in the [past](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Serbia_(1718%E2%80%931739) "Kingdom of Serbia (1718–1739)"), as [occupation of 1788–1791](/wiki/Habsburg-occupied_Serbia_(1788%E2%80%931791) "Habsburg-occupied Serbia (1788–1791)") was still a fresh memory. However, the Austrian authorities, due to difficulties with Napoleon and because they wanted to maintain their neutrality in order to be correct with the Porte, could not accept his offers. So, the Serbs were forced to ask for the protection of the Russians, and therefore, on 3 May 1804, the Serb leaders sent a letter to the Russian envoy in [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), in which they spoke of the problems and wishes of the Serbian people, but they also stressed that they would continue to be loyal to the Sultan. Due to the recent [Russo-Ottoman alliance](/wiki/Russo-Ottoman_alliance "Russo-Ottoman alliance") against France's expanding influence in the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans") (the approach of French troops to the Turkish territories, like [occupation of Corfu and other Ionian islands in 1797–99](/wiki/French_rule_in_the_Ionian_Islands_(1797%E2%80%931799) "French rule in the Ionian Islands (1797–1799)"), influenced the Porte to conclude an alliance with Russia in 1798), the Russian government had a neutral policy toward the Serbian revolt until summer 1804, in which the goal was now to having Constantinople recognize Russia as the guarantor of peace in the region.[[99]](#cite_note-120)

In 1806, the Serbs rejected [Ičko's Peace](/wiki/I%C4%8Dko%27s_Peace "Ičko's Peace") (the Ottomans seemed ready to grant [Serbia](/wiki/Ottoman_Serbia "Ottoman Serbia") autonomy, similar to that enjoyed by neighbouring [Wallachia](/wiki/Wallachia "Wallachia"), in order to enter in the Napoleonic Wars as an ally of the French) as they desired Russian support for their independence, starting a new phase of the [uprising](/wiki/First_Serbian_Uprising "First Serbian Uprising") in which the Serbs planned to create their own [national state](/wiki/Nation_state "Nation state"), which would also include the territories of [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"), as well as the pashaliks of [Vidin](/wiki/Vidin "Vidin"), [Nis](/wiki/Nis_(Serbia) "Nis (Serbia)"), [Leskovac](/wiki/Leskovac "Leskovac"), and [Pazar](/wiki/Pazardzhik_Province "Pazardzhik Province"). Also, in the [Traditionalist](/wiki/Traditionalist_conservatism "Traditionalist conservatism") circles of Serbian rebels,[[100]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-121) [Petar I](/wiki/Petar_I_Petrovi%C4%87-Njego%C5%A1 "Petar I Petrović-Njegoš") of [Montenegro](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Montenegro "Prince-Bishopric of Montenegro") developed a plan in 1807 to restore the medieval [Serbian Empire](/wiki/Serbian_Empire "Serbian Empire") ("Slaveno–Serb empire"), consisting on unify [Podgorica](/wiki/Podgorica "Podgorica"), [Spuž](/wiki/Spu%C5%BE "Spuž"), [Žabljak](/wiki/%C5%BDabljak "Žabljak"), the [Bay of Kotor](/wiki/Bay_of_Kotor "Bay of Kotor"), [Bosnia](/wiki/Bosnia_(region) "Bosnia (region)"), [Herzegovina](/wiki/Herzegovina "Herzegovina"), [Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik") and [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia") with [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro"),[[101]](#cite_note-V-1_pp._170-122) which he informed the Russian court[[101]](#cite_note-V-1_pp._170-122)[[100]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-121)[[102]](#cite_note-123)[[103]](#cite_note-124) and was also viewed by [Habsburg Serb](/wiki/Habsburg_Serbs "Habsburg Serbs") metropolitan [Stefan Stratimirović](/wiki/Stefan_Stratimirovi%C4%87 "Stefan Stratimirović").[[104]](#cite_note-125) The title of [Emperor of the Serbs](/wiki/Emperor_of_the_Serbs "Emperor of the Serbs") would be held by the [Russian emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_Russia "Emperor of Russia") as [Tsar](/wiki/Tsar "Tsar"),[[101]](#cite_note-V-1_pp._170-122) but with the condition that Russians respected the [independence-autocephaly](/wiki/Autocephaly "Autocephaly") of the [Montenegrin Orthodox Church](/wiki/Montenegrin_Orthodox_Church "Montenegrin Orthodox Church"). So, in February 1807, Petar I planned an invasion of [Herzegovina](/wiki/Herzegovina "Herzegovina") by [Montenegrin](/wiki/Montenegrins "Montenegrins") forces and asked for Karađorđe's aid,[[105]](#cite_note-126) wanting to connect the territory occupied by the Serbian rebel forces and Montenegro, which succeeded after the [Battle of Suvodol](/wiki/Battle_of_Suvodol "Battle of Suvodol") in 1809.[[106]](#cite_note-127) This support of Montenegro for the Serbs was reinforced due to the fact that in the war of 1806–1812, Ottoman troops, supported by French detachments on Illyria, attacked Montenegro along the entire border, and the Montenegrins did not have time to repel all the attacks.
