However, they managed to force the French troops to withdraw from [Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik") and conquered the [Bay of Kotor](/wiki/Bay_of_Kotor "Bay of Kotor"). Napoleon himself offered Petar I the title "Patriarch of the entire Serbian nation or of the entire Illyricum" on the condition that he cease cooperation with Russia and accept a French protectorate, which he refused for fear of an eventual papal status jurisdiction or an [Anti-clerical](/wiki/Anti-clericalism "Anti-clericalism") policy.[[100]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-121)[[107]](#cite_note-montenegrina.net-128)

However, the [Treaty of Tilsit](/wiki/Treaties_of_Tilsit "Treaties of Tilsit") between France and Russia against the Ottomans helped put a stop to hostilities in the Balkans, with a truce taking place (which was perceived extremely negatively in Serbia, despite the fact that the truce did not apply to the Serb rebels). Also, there was a secret clause that provided for the division of Turkish possessions in the Balkans between Russia and France[[108]](#cite_note-129) and the cancellation of the Slaveno-Serb empire project.[[101]](#cite_note-V-1_pp._170-122) In 1809, [Karađorđe](/wiki/Kara%C4%91or%C4%91e "Karađorđe") appealed to an alliance with the [Habsburgs](/wiki/Habsburgs "Habsburgs") and Napoleon, with no success,[[109]](#cite_note-130) even wrote personally to Napoleon seeking military assistance, and in 1810, dispatched an emissary to the French Empire.[[110]](#cite_note-131) However, the French did not believe that the rebels had the military capacity to defeat the Ottomans or expulse them from the Balkans. In 1812, under pressure from Napoleon, Russia was forced to sign the [Treaty of Bucharest](/wiki/Treaty_of_Bucharest_(1812) "Treaty of Bucharest (1812)"), which restored peace with the Ottomans.[[111]](#cite_note-132) One of the clauses of the treaty provided for the maintenance of Serbian autonomy, also, a truce was signed according to the Article 8 of the Treaty. Then, the Russians encouraged Karađorđe and his followers to negotiate directly with the Porte.[[112]](#cite_note-133)[[113]](#cite_note-134)

### War of 1812

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: War of 1812")]

Main article: [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812")

See also: [The United States and the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/The_United_States_and_the_French_Revolutionary_and_Napoleonic_Wars "The United States and the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Battle_of_Lundys_Lane.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Lundys_Lane.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Lundys_Lane.jpg)

The [Battle of Lundy's Lane](/wiki/Battle_of_Lundy%27s_Lane "Battle of Lundy's Lane") shown here, was a hard-fought battle that was a tactical draw, but it forced the Americans to withdraw during the [Niagara campaign](/wiki/Niagara_campaign "Niagara campaign").

The [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812") coincided with the War of the Sixth Coalition. Historians in the United States and Canada see it as a war in its own right, while Europeans often see it as a minor theatre of the Napoleonic Wars. The United States declared war on Britain due to a variety of reasons, including British support for [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States") resisting US settler expansion, [interference](/wiki/Orders_in_Council_(1807) "Orders in Council (1807)") with American merchant shipping, the [impressment](/wiki/Impressment "Impressment") of Royal Navy deserters from US merchantmen, and [expansionist](/wiki/Expansionism "Expansionism") American desires to occupy [Canada](/wiki/The_Canadas "The Canadas"). France had interfered with American shipping as well, and the United States had considered declaring war on France. The war ended in a military stalemate under the [Treaty of Ghent](/wiki/Treaty_of_Ghent "Treaty of Ghent"), which took effect in early 1815 when Napoleon was still in Elba and returned the pre-war boundaries.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBlack2009-135)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Latin American Revolutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Latin American Revolutions")]

Main articles: [Spanish American wars of independence](/wiki/Spanish_American_wars_of_independence "Spanish American wars of independence"), [Brazilian War of Independence](/wiki/Brazilian_War_of_Independence "Brazilian War of Independence"), and [Haitian Revolution](/wiki/Haitian_Revolution "Haitian Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/America_1794.png/250px-America_1794.png)](/wiki/File:America_1794.png)

Political map of the Americas in 1794

The abdication of Kings [Charles IV of Spain](/wiki/Charles_IV_of_Spain "Charles IV of Spain") and [Ferdinand VII](/wiki/Ferdinand_VII "Ferdinand VII") of Spain and the installation of Napoleon's brother as King [José](/wiki/Joseph_Bonaparte "Joseph Bonaparte") provoked civil wars and revolutions, leading to the independence of most of Spain's mainland American colonies. In [Spanish America](/wiki/Hispanic_America "Hispanic America"), many local elites formed juntas and set up mechanisms to rule in the name of Ferdinand VII, whom they considered the legitimate Spanish monarch. The outbreak of the [Spanish American wars of independence](/wiki/Spanish_American_wars_of_independence "Spanish American wars of independence") in most of the empire was a result of Napoleon's destabilizing actions in Spain, and led to the rise of [strongmen](/wiki/Strongman_(politics) "Strongman (politics)") in the wake of these wars.[[115]](#cite_note-136) The defeat of Napoleon at Waterloo in 1815 caused an exodus of French soldiers into Latin America, where they joined ranks with the armies of the independence movements.[[116]](#cite_note-OreBerg18-137) While these officials had a role in various victories such as the [Capture of Valdivia](/wiki/Capture_of_Valdivia "Capture of Valdivia") (1820), some are held responsible for significant defeats at the hands of the royalists, as was the case at the [Second Battle of Cancha Rayada](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Cancha_Rayada "Second Battle of Cancha Rayada") (1818).[[116]](#cite_note-OreBerg18-137)

In contrast, the Portuguese [royal family](/wiki/House_of_Braganza "House of Braganza") escaped to [Brazil](/wiki/Colonial_Brazil "Colonial Brazil") and established the court there, resulting in political stability for Portuguese America. In 1816, Brazil was proclaimed an equal part of the [United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves"), paving the way to Brazilian independence six years later.

The [Haitian Revolution](/wiki/Haitian_Revolution "Haitian Revolution") began in 1791, just before the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars"), and continued until 1804. France's defeat resulted in the independence of [Saint-Domingue](/wiki/Saint-Domingue "Saint-Domingue") and led Napoleon to sell the territory making up the [Louisiana Purchase](/wiki/Louisiana_Purchase "Louisiana Purchase") to the United States.[[117]](#cite_note-138)

### Barbary Wars

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Barbary Wars")]

Main article: [Barbary Wars](/wiki/Barbary_Wars "Barbary Wars")

During the Napoleonic Wars, the United States, Sweden, and Sicily fought against the [Barbary pirates](/wiki/Barbary_pirates "Barbary pirates") in the Mediterranean. The Barbary wars were a conflict between the United States and the North African States (Tripoli, Algiers, Tunis, and Morocco).

## Invasion of Russia, 1812

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Invasion of Russia, 1812")]

Main article: [French invasion of Russia](/wiki/French_invasion_of_Russia "French invasion of Russia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Battle_of_Borodino.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Borodino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Borodino.jpg)

The [Battle of Borodino](/wiki/Battle_of_Borodino "Battle of Borodino") as depicted by [Louis Lejeune](/wiki/Louis-Fran%C3%A7ois,_Baron_Lejeune "Louis-François, Baron Lejeune"). The battle was the largest and bloodiest single-day action of the Napoleonic Wars.

The [Treaty of Tilsit](/wiki/Treaty_of_Tilsit "Treaty of Tilsit") in 1807 resulted in the [Anglo–Russian War](/wiki/Anglo-Russian_War_(1807%E2%80%931812) "Anglo-Russian War (1807–1812)") (1807–1812). Emperor [Alexander I](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Russia "Alexander I of Russia") declared war on Britain after the British attack on Denmark in September 1807. British men-of-war supported the Swedish fleet during the [Finnish War](/wiki/Finnish_War "Finnish War") and won victories over the Russians in the [Gulf of Finland](/wiki/Gulf_of_Finland "Gulf of Finland") in July 1808 and August 1809. The success of the Russian army on land, however, forced Sweden to sign peace treaties with Russia in 1809 and with France in 1810, and to join the blockade against Britain. But Franco–Russian relations would become progressively worse after 1810, and the Russian war with Britain effectively ended. In April 1812, Britain, Russia, and Sweden signed secret agreements directed against Napoleon.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer1974-139)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

The central issue for both [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_the_French "Emperor of the French") [Napoleon I](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") and [Tsar](/wiki/Emperor_of_Russia "Emperor of Russia") [Alexander I](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Russia "Alexander I of Russia") was control over Poland. Each wanted a semi-independent Poland he could control. As Esdaile notes, "Implicit in the idea of a Russian Poland was, of course, a war against Napoleon."[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEsdaile2009438-140) Schroeder says Poland was "the root cause" of Napoleon's war with Russia, but Russia's refusal to support the Continental System was also a factor.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchroeder1994419-141)

In 1812, at the height of his power, Napoleon invaded Russia with a pan-European *[Grande Armée](/wiki/Grande_Arm%C3%A9e "Grande Armée")*, consisting of 450,000 men (200,000 Frenchmen, and many soldiers of allies or subject areas). The French forces crossed the [Niemen](/wiki/Neman "Neman") river on 24 June 1812. Russia proclaimed a Patriotic War, and Napoleon proclaimed a Second Polish war. The Poles supplied almost 100,000 men for the invasion force, but against their expectations, Napoleon avoided any concessions to Poland, having in mind further negotiations with Russia.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiehn1990-142)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

The *Grande Armée* marched through Russia, winning some relatively minor engagements and the major [Battle of Smolensk](/wiki/Battle_of_Smolensk_(1812) "Battle of Smolensk (1812)") on 16–18 August. In the same days, part of the French Army led by [Marshal](/wiki/Marshal_of_France "Marshal of France") [Nicolas Oudinot](/wiki/Nicolas_Oudinot "Nicolas Oudinot") was stopped in the [Battle of Polotsk](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Polotsk "First Battle of Polotsk") by the right wing of the Russian Army, under command of General [Peter Wittgenstein](/wiki/Peter_Wittgenstein "Peter Wittgenstein"). This prevented the French march on the Russian capital, [Saint Petersburg](/wiki/Saint_Petersburg "Saint Petersburg"); the fate of the invasion was decided in Moscow, where Napoleon led his forces in person.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Napoleons_retreat_from_Moscow_by_Adolph_Northen.jpg/250px-Napoleons_retreat_from_Moscow_by_Adolph_Northen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Napoleons_retreat_from_Moscow_by_Adolph_Northen.jpg)

*[Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s withdrawal from Russia*, a painting by [Adolph Northen](/wiki/Adolph_Northen "Adolph Northen")

The main [Imperial Russian Army](/wiki/Imperial_Russian_Army "Imperial Russian Army") was commanded by [Field Marshal](/wiki/Field_Marshal "Field Marshal") [Michael Andreas Barclay de Tolly](/wiki/Michael_Andreas_Barclay_de_Tolly "Michael Andreas Barclay de Tolly"), who recognized that Napoleon's immediate goal was a decisive battle to crush the main Russian force in the west. In response, the Russian army used [scorched-earth](/wiki/Scorched-earth "Scorched-earth") tactics as it withdrew east, and harried the *Grande Armée* with light [Cossack](/wiki/Cossack "Cossack") cavalry. The *Grande Armée* did not adjust its operational methods in response.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiehn1990138–140-143) Provisioning such an enormous army with adequate food and fresh water had proven difficult since the very start of the campaign, exacerbated by the sparse terrain of [western Russia](/wiki/Western_Russia "Western Russia"); diseases such as [typhus](/wiki/Typhus "Typhus") and [dysentery](/wiki/Dysentery "Dysentery") rapidly became rampant among the rank and file. These factors led to most of the losses of the main column of the *Grande Armée*, which in one case amounted to 95,000 men, including deserters, in a week.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiehn1990185-144)

The main Russian army retreated for almost three months. This constant retreat led to the increasing unpopularity of Barclay de Tolly (who was a [Baltic German](/wiki/Baltic_German "Baltic German") and already mistrusted by the Russian elite) and a respected Russian veteran, Prince [Mikhail Kutuzov](/wiki/Mikhail_Kutuzov "Mikhail Kutuzov"), was made the new Commander-in-Chief by Tsar Alexander. Finally, the two armies engaged in the [Battle of Borodino](/wiki/Battle_of_Borodino "Battle of Borodino") on 7 September,[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaythornthwaite2012-145)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] in the vicinity of Moscow. The battle was the largest and bloodiest single-day action of the Napoleonic Wars, involving more than 250,000 men and resulting in at least 70,000 casualties.

The battle was indecisive; the French captured the main positions on the battlefield but failed to destroy the Russian army. While Kutuzov had recognized the political need to give battle, he also recognized that Barclay de Tolly's strategies had proven effective, and that the *Grande Armée* was bleeding personnel and supplies more and more severely with every step they took deeper into Russia. Napoleon's logistical difficulties meant that French casualties simply could not be replaced, whereas the Russian army could reinforce itself far more readily.

[Napoleon I](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") entered [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow") on 14 September, after the Russian Army had retreated yet again.[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiehn1990253–254-146) By then, the Russians had largely evacuated the city and released criminals from the prisons to inconvenience the French; the governor, Count [Fyodor Rostopchin](/wiki/Fyodor_Rostopchin "Fyodor Rostopchin"), ordered the city [to be burnt](/wiki/Fire_of_Moscow_(1812) "Fire of Moscow (1812)").[[126]](#cite_note-147) Alexander I refused to capitulate, and the peace talks attempted by Napoleon failed. Rather than withdraw further east, the Russian army withdrew south, rebuilding its strength and preparing to interfere with a French withdrawal. In October, with no sign of clear victory in sight, Napoleon began the disastrous Great Retreat from Moscow.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Minard%27s_Map_%28vectorized%29.svg/500px-Minard%27s_Map_%28vectorized%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Minard%27s_Map_(vectorized).svg)

[Charles Joseph Minard](/wiki/Charles_Joseph_Minard "Charles Joseph Minard")'s graph of the decreasing size of the *Grande Armée* represented by the width of the line as it marches to Moscow (tan) and back (black)

The French tried to reach [Kaluga](/wiki/Kaluga "Kaluga") and swing through southern Russia, where they could find food and forage supplies. In the [Battle of Maloyaroslavets](/wiki/Battle_of_Maloyaroslavets "Battle of Maloyaroslavets"), the replenished Russian army blocked the road to Kaluga. While an indecisive engagement, it nevertheless doomed the *Grande Armée*; with the Russians refusing to be dislodged, Napoleon was forced to retreat back down the [Smolensk](/wiki/Smolensk "Smolensk") road along which he had already advanced, and which had already been denuded of supplies—most crucially, food.

The French supply chain, already in tatters from the attrition of wagons and horses, began to collapse completely. The lack of horses also rendered Napoleon's cavalry ineffective, leaving the *Grande Armée* vulnerable to sustained [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/Guerrilla_warfare "Guerrilla warfare") by Russian peasants and irregular troops. The *Grande Armée* was dealt a further catastrophic blow by the onset of the [Russian Winter](/wiki/Russian_Winter "Russian Winter"), which the underfed and undersupplied *Grande Armée* was unprepared to cope with.

When the remnants of Napoleon's army [struggled across the Berezina River](/wiki/Battle_of_Berezina "Battle of Berezina") in November with the Russian army in pursuit, only 27,000 fit soldiers survived, with 380,000 men dead or missing and 100,000 captured.[[127]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUpshall199317-148) Napoleon then left his men and returned to Paris to prepare the defence against the advancing Russians. The campaign had effectively ended on 14 December 1812, when the last enemy troops left Russia. The Russians had lost around 210,000 men, but with their shorter supply lines, they soon replenished their armies. For every six soldiers of the *Grande Armée* that entered Russia, only one would make it out in fighting condition.

## War of the Sixth Coalition, 1812–1814

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: War of the Sixth Coalition, 1812–1814")]

Main article: [War of the Sixth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Sixth_Coalition "War of the Sixth Coalition")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Archive-ugent-be-7D309E20-C7E9-11E0-A4B4-E65737D8FA8C_DS-9_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Archive-ugent-be-7D309E20-C7E9-11E0-A4B4-E65737D8FA8C_DS-9_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Archive-ugent-be-7D309E20-C7E9-11E0-A4B4-E65737D8FA8C_DS-9_(cropped).jpg)

Fragment from the manuscript "Memoires on [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s campaigns, experienced as a soldier of the second regiment". Written by [Joseph Abbeel](/wiki/Joseph_Abbeel "Joseph Abbeel"), a soldier participating in the War of the Sixth Coalition, 1805–1815.[[128]](#cite_note-149)

Seeing an opportunity in [Napoleon I](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon")'s historic defeat, [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") and several other German states switched sides, joining [Russia](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") and others opposing Napoleon.[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDwyer2013431–474-150)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Napoleon vowed that he would create a new army as large as the one he had sent into Russia, and quickly built up his forces in the east from 30,000 to 130,000 and eventually to 400,000. Napoleon inflicted 40,000 casualties on the Allies at [Lützen](/wiki/Battle_of_L%C3%BCtzen_(1813) "Battle of Lützen (1813)") (2 May 1813) and [Bautzen](/wiki/Battle_of_Bautzen_(1813) "Battle of Bautzen (1813)") (20–21 May 1813). Both battles involved forces of over 250,000, making them some of the largest conflicts of the wars so far. [Klemens von Metternich](/wiki/Klemens_von_Metternich "Klemens von Metternich") in November 1813 offered Napoleon the [Frankfurt proposals](/wiki/Frankfurt_proposals "Frankfurt proposals"). They would allow [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") to remain [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_of_the_French "Emperor of the French") but [France](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") would be reduced to its "natural frontiers" and lose control of most of Italy and Germany and the Netherlands. Napoleon still expected to win the wars, and rejected the terms. By 1814, as the Allies were closing in on [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), [Napoleon I](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") did agree to the Frankfurt proposals, but it was too late and he rejected the new harsher terms proposed by the Allies.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiley2013206-151)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/MoshkovVI_SrazhLeypcigomGRM.jpg/250px-MoshkovVI_SrazhLeypcigomGRM.jpg)](/wiki/File:MoshkovVI_SrazhLeypcigomGRM.jpg)

The [Battle of Leipzig](/wiki/Battle_of_Leipzig "Battle of Leipzig") involved over 600,000 soldiers, making it the largest battle in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") prior to [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").

In the [Peninsular War](/wiki/Peninsular_War "Peninsular War"), [Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington](/wiki/Arthur_Wellesley,_1st_Duke_of_Wellington "Arthur Wellesley, 1st Duke of Wellington"), renewed the [Anglo-Portuguese](/wiki/Anglo-Portuguese_Army "Anglo-Portuguese Army") advance into Spain just after New Year in 1812, besieging and capturing the fortified towns of [Ciudad Rodrigo](/wiki/Siege_of_Ciudad_Rodrigo_(1812) "Siege of Ciudad Rodrigo (1812)"), [Badajoz](/wiki/Siege_of_Badajoz_(1812) "Siege of Badajoz (1812)"), and crushing a French army at the [Battle of Salamanca](/wiki/Battle_of_Salamanca "Battle of Salamanca"). As the French regrouped, the Anglo-Portuguese entered Madrid and advanced towards [Burgos](/wiki/Siege_of_Burgos "Siege of Burgos"), before retreating all the way to Portugal when renewed French concentrations threatened to trap them. As a consequence of the Salamanca campaign, the French were forced to end their long siege of Cádiz and to permanently evacuate the provinces of [Andalusia](/wiki/Andalusia "Andalusia") and [Asturias](/wiki/Asturias "Asturias").[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYoungLawford2015-152)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

In a strategic move, Wellesley planned to move his supply base from Lisbon to [Santander](/wiki/Santander,_Spain "Santander, Spain"). The Anglo-Portuguese forces swept northwards in late May and seized Burgos. On 21 June, at [Vitoria](/wiki/Battle_of_Vitoria "Battle of Vitoria"), the combined Anglo-Portuguese and Spanish armies won against [Joseph Bonaparte](/wiki/Joseph_Bonaparte "Joseph Bonaparte"), finally breaking French power in Spain. The French had to retreat from the Iberian peninsula, over the [Pyrenees](/wiki/Pyrenees "Pyrenees").[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGlover1963-153)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

The belligerents declared an armistice from 4 June 1813 (continuing until 13 August) during which time both sides attempted to recover from the loss of approximately a quarter of a million men in the preceding two months. During this time coalition negotiations finally brought Austria out in open opposition to France. Two principal Austrian armies took the field, adding 300,000 men to the coalition armies in Germany. The Allies now had around 800,000 front-line soldiers in the German theatre, with a strategic reserve of 350,000 formed to support the front-line operations.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiley2013206-151)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/General_Nansouty_at_the_battle_of_Hanau.jpg/250px-General_Nansouty_at_the_battle_of_Hanau.jpg)](/wiki/File:General_Nansouty_at_the_battle_of_Hanau.jpg)

The [Battle of Hanau](/wiki/Battle_of_Hanau "Battle of Hanau") (30–31 October 1813) was fought between Austro-Bavarian and French forces.

Napoleon succeeded in bringing the imperial forces in the region to around 650,000—although only 250,000 came under his direct command, with another 120,000 under [Nicolas Charles Oudinot](/wiki/Nicolas_Charles_Oudinot "Nicolas Charles Oudinot") and 30,000 under Davout. The remaining imperial forces came mostly from the Confederation of the Rhine, especially Saxony and Bavaria. In addition, to the south, Murat's Kingdom of Naples and [Eugène de Beauharnais](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_de_Beauharnais "Eugène de Beauharnais")'s Kingdom of Italy had 100,000 armed men. In Spain, another 150,000 to 200,000 French troops steadily retreated before Anglo-Portuguese forces numbering around 100,000. Thus around 900,000 Frenchmen in all theatres faced around 1,800,000 coalition soldiers (including the strategic reserve under formation in Germany). The gross figures may mislead slightly, as most of the German troops fighting on the side of the French fought at best unreliably and stood on the verge of defecting to the Allies. One can reasonably say that Napoleon could count on no more than 450,000 men in Germany—which left him outnumbered about four to one.[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERiley2013206-151)

Following the end of the armistice, Napoleon seemed to have regained the initiative at [Dresden](/wiki/Battle_of_Dresden "Battle of Dresden") (August 1813), where he once again defeated a numerically superior coalition army and inflicted enormous casualties, while sustaining relatively few. The failures of his marshals and a slow resumption of the offensive on his part cost him any advantage that this victory might have secured. At the [Battle of Leipzig](/wiki/Battle_of_Leipzig "Battle of Leipzig") in Saxony (16–19 October 1813), also called the "Battle of the Nations", 191,000 French fought more than 300,000 Allies, and the defeated French had to retreat into France. After the French withdrawal from Germany, Napoleon's remaining ally, [Denmark–Norway](/wiki/Denmark%E2%80%93Norway "Denmark–Norway"), became isolated and [fell to the coalition](/wiki/Treaty_of_Kiel "Treaty of Kiel").[[133]](#cite_note-154)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Russparis.jpg/250px-Russparis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Russparis.jpg)

Russian army enters Paris, 31 March 1814

Napoleon then fought a series of battles in France, including the [Battle of Arcis-sur-Aube](/wiki/Battle_of_Arcis-sur-Aube "Battle of Arcis-sur-Aube"), but the overwhelming numbers of the Allies steadily forced him back. The Allies [entered Paris](/wiki/Battle_of_Paris_(1814) "Battle of Paris (1814)") on 30 March 1814. During this time Napoleon fought his [Six Days' Campaign](/wiki/Six_Days%27_Campaign "Six Days' Campaign"), in which he won many battles against the enemy forces advancing towards Paris. During this entire campaign, he never managed to field more than 70,000 men against more than half a million coalition soldiers. At the [Treaty of Chaumont](/wiki/Treaty_of_Chaumont "Treaty of Chaumont") (9 March 1814), the Allies agreed to preserve the coalition until Napoleon's total defeat.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDwyer2013464–498-155)

Napoleon determined to fight on, even now, incapable of fathoming his fall from power. During the campaign, he had issued a decree for 900,000 fresh conscripts, but only a fraction of these materialised, and Napoleon's schemes for victory eventually gave way to the reality of his hopeless situation. Napoleon abdicated on 6 April. Occasional military actions continued in Italy, Spain, and Holland in early 1814.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDwyer2013464–498-155) An armistice was signed with the Allied Powers on 23 April 1814. The [First Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1814) "Treaty of Paris (1814)"), signed on 30 May 1814, officially ended the War of the Sixth Coalition.

The victors exiled Napoleon to the island of [Elba](/wiki/Elba "Elba") and restored the French [Bourbon](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon "House of Bourbon") monarchy in the person of [Louis XVIII](/wiki/Louis_XVIII "Louis XVIII"). They signed the [Treaty of Fontainebleau](/wiki/Treaty_of_Fontainebleau_(1814) "Treaty of Fontainebleau (1814)") (11 April 1814) and initiated the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") to redraw the map of Europe.[[134]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDwyer2013464–498-155)

## War of the Seventh Coalition, 1815

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: War of the Seventh Coalition, 1815")]

See also: [Hundred Days](/wiki/Hundred_Days "Hundred Days") and [Neapolitan War](/wiki/Neapolitan_War "Neapolitan War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Wellington_at_Waterloo_Hillingford.jpg/250px-Wellington_at_Waterloo_Hillingford.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wellington_at_Waterloo_Hillingford.jpg)

*Wellington at Waterloo* by [Robert Alexander Hillingford](/wiki/Robert_Alexander_Hillingford "Robert Alexander Hillingford")

The [Seventh Coalition](/wiki/Seventh_Coalition "Seventh Coalition") (1815) pitted Britain, Russia, Prussia, Sweden, Switzerland, Austria, the [Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands") and several smaller German states against France. The period known as the Hundred Days began after Napoleon escaped from Elba and landed at [Cannes](/wiki/Cannes "Cannes") (1 March 1815). Travelling to Paris, picking up support as he went, he eventually overthrew [Louis XVIII](/wiki/Louis_XVIII "Louis XVIII"). The Allies rapidly gathered their armies to meet him again. Napoleon raised 280,000 men, whom he distributed among several armies. To add to the 90,000-strong standing army, he recalled well over a quarter of a million veterans from past campaigns and issued a decree for the eventual draft of around 2.5 million new men into the French army, which was never achieved. This faced an initial coalition force of about 700,000—although coalition campaign plans provided for one million front-line soldiers, supported by around 200,000 garrison, logistics and other auxiliary personnel.

Napoleon took about 124,000 men of the Army of the North on a pre-emptive strike against the Allies in Belgium.[[135]](#cite_note-156) He intended to attack the coalition armies before they combined, in hope of driving the British into the sea and the Prussians out of the war. His march to the frontier achieved the surprise he had planned, catching the Anglo-Dutch Army in a dispersed arrangement. The Prussians had been more wary, concentrating 75 per cent of their army in and around [Ligny](/wiki/Ligny "Ligny"). The Prussians forced the *[Armée du Nord](/wiki/Arm%C3%A9e_du_Nord "Armée du Nord")* to fight all the day of the 15th to reach Ligny in a delaying action by the Prussian 1st Corps. He forced Prussia to fight at [Ligny](/wiki/Battle_of_Ligny "Battle of Ligny") on 16 June 1815, and the defeated Prussians retreated in disorder. On the same day, the left wing of the *Armée du Nord*, under the command of Marshal [Michel Ney](/wiki/Michel_Ney "Michel Ney"), succeeded in stopping any of Wellington's forces going to aid [Blücher](/wiki/Gebhard_Leberecht_von_Bl%C3%BCcher "Gebhard Leberecht von Blücher")'s Prussians by fighting a blocking action at [Quatre Bras](/wiki/Battle_of_Quatre_Bras "Battle of Quatre Bras"). Ney failed to clear the cross-roads and Wellington reinforced the position. But with the Prussian retreat, Wellington too had to retreat. He fell back to a previously reconnoitred position on an [escarpment](/wiki/Escarpment "Escarpment") at [Mont St Jean](/wiki/Mont-Saint-Jean,_Belgium "Mont-Saint-Jean, Belgium"), a few miles south of the village of [Waterloo](/wiki/Waterloo,_Belgium "Waterloo, Belgium").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Waterloo_Campaign_map-alt3.svg/500px-Waterloo_Campaign_map-alt3.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Waterloo_Campaign_map-alt3.svg)

Map of the Waterloo campaign

Napoleon took the reserve of the Army of the North, and reunited his forces with those of Ney to pursue Wellington's army, after he ordered Marshal [Grouchy](/wiki/Emmanuel,_marquis_de_Grouchy "Emmanuel, marquis de Grouchy") to take the right wing of the Army of the North and stop the Prussians regrouping. In the first of a series of miscalculations, both Grouchy and Napoleon failed to realise that the Prussian forces were already reorganised and were assembling at the city of [Wavre](/wiki/Wavre "Wavre"). The French army did nothing to stop a rather leisurely retreat that took place throughout the night and into the early morning by the Prussians. As the 4th, 1st, and 2nd Prussian Corps marched through the town towards Waterloo, the 3rd Prussian Corps took up blocking positions across the river, and although Grouchy engaged and defeated the Prussian rearguard under the command of Lt-Gen [von Thielmann](/wiki/Johann_von_Thielmann "Johann von Thielmann") in the [Battle of Wavre](/wiki/Battle_of_Wavre "Battle of Wavre") (18–19 June) it was 12 hours too late. In the end, 17,000 Prussians had kept 33,000 badly needed French reinforcements off the field.

Napoleon delayed the start of fighting at the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") on the morning of 18 June for several hours while he waited for the ground to dry after the previous night's rain. By late afternoon, the French army had not succeeded in driving Wellington's forces from the escarpment on which they stood. When the Prussians arrived and attacked the French right flank in ever-increasing numbers, Napoleon's strategy of keeping the coalition armies divided had failed and a combined coalition general advance drove his army from the field in confusion.[[136]](#cite_note-157)

Grouchy organised a successful and well-ordered retreat towards Paris, where Marshal Davout had 117,000 men ready to turn back the 116,000 men of Blücher and Wellington. General [Vandamme](/wiki/Dominique_Vandamme "Dominique Vandamme") was defeated at the [Battle of Issy](/wiki/Battle_of_Issy "Battle of Issy") and negotiations for surrender had begun.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Charge_of_the_French_Cuirassiers_at_Waterloo.jpg/330px-Charge_of_the_French_Cuirassiers_at_Waterloo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Charge_of_the_French_Cuirassiers_at_Waterloo.jpg)

The charge of the French [Cuirassiers](/wiki/Cuirassier "Cuirassier") at the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") against a square of [Scottish Highlanders](/wiki/Scottish_regiment "Scottish regiment")

On arriving at Paris three days after Waterloo, Napoleon still clung to the hope of a concerted national resistance; but the temper of the [legislative chambers](/wiki/Corps_l%C3%A9gislatif "Corps législatif"), and of the public generally, did not favour his view. Lacking support Napoleon abdicated again on 22 June 1815, and on 15 July he surrendered to the British squadron at [Rochefort](/wiki/Rochefort,_Charente-Maritime "Rochefort, Charente-Maritime"). The Allies exiled him to the remote South Atlantic island of [Saint Helena](/wiki/Saint_Helena "Saint Helena"), where he died on 5 May 1821.

In Italy, [Joachim Murat](/wiki/Joachim_Murat "Joachim Murat"), whom the Allies had allowed to remain King of [Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Naples (Napoleonic)") after Napoleon's initial defeat, once again allied with his brother-in-law, triggering the [Neapolitan War](/wiki/Neapolitan_War "Neapolitan War") (March to May 1815). Hoping to find support among Italian nationalists fearing the increasing influence of the Habsburgs in Italy, Murat issued the [Rimini Proclamation](/wiki/Rimini_Proclamation "Rimini Proclamation") inciting them to war. The proclamation failed and the Austrians soon crushed Murat at the [Battle of Tolentino](/wiki/Battle_of_Tolentino "Battle of Tolentino") (2–3 May 1815), forcing him to flee. The [Bourbons](/wiki/House_of_Bourbon-Two_Sicilies "House of Bourbon-Two Sicilies") returned to the throne of Naples on 20 May 1815. Murat tried to regain his throne, but after that failed, he was executed by firing squad on 13 October 1815.

The [Second Treaty of Paris](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_(1815) "Treaty of Paris (1815)"), signed on 20 November 1815, officially marked the end of the Napoleonic Wars.[[137]](#cite_note-158)

## Political effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Political effects")]

The Napoleonic Wars brought radical changes to Europe, but the [reactionary](/wiki/Reactionary "Reactionary") forces returned and [restored the Bourbon house](/wiki/Bourbon_Restoration_in_France "Bourbon Restoration in France") to the French throne. Napoleon had succeeded in bringing most of Western Europe under one rule. In most European countries, subjugation in the French Empire brought with it many liberal features of the French Revolution including democracy, [due process](/wiki/Due_process "Due process") in courts, abolition of [serfdom](/wiki/Serfdom "Serfdom"), reduction of the power of the Catholic Church, and demand for constitutional limits on monarchs. The increasing voice of the [middle classes](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class") with rising commerce and industry meant that restored European monarchs found it difficult to restore pre-revolutionary [absolutism](/wiki/Absolute_monarch "Absolute monarch") and had to retain many of the reforms enacted during Napoleon's rule. Institutional legacies remain to this day in the form of [civil law](/wiki/Civil_Law_(legal_system) "Civil Law (legal system)"), with clearly defined [codes of law](/wiki/Code_of_law "Code of law")—an enduring legacy of the [Napoleonic Code](/wiki/Napoleonic_Code "Napoleonic Code").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Europe_1815_map_en.png/500px-Europe_1815_map_en.png)](/wiki/File:Europe_1815_map_en.png)

The national boundaries within Europe set by the Congress of Vienna, 1815

France's constant warfare with the combined forces of different combinations of, and eventually all, of the other major powers of Europe for over two decades finally took its toll. By the end of the Napoleonic Wars, France no longer held the role of the dominant [power](/wiki/Historical_powers "Historical powers") in Continental Europe, as it had since the times of [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"), as the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") produced a "[balance of power](/wiki/Balance_of_power_(international_relations) "Balance of power (international relations)")" by resizing the main powers so they could balance each other and remain at peace. In this regard, [Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") was restored in its former borders, and also received large chunks of [Poland](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Warsaw "Grand Duchy of Warsaw") and [Saxony](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Saxony "Kingdom of Saxony"). Greatly enlarged, Prussia became a permanent [Great Power](/wiki/Great_Power "Great Power"). In order to drag Prussia's attention towards the west and France, the Congress also gave the [Rhineland](/wiki/Prussian_Rhineland "Prussian Rhineland") and [Westphalia](/wiki/Prussian_Westphalia "Prussian Westphalia") to Prussia. These industrial regions transformed agrarian Prussia into an industrial leader in the nineteenth century.[[138]](#cite_note-auto3-159) Britain emerged as the most important economic power, and its [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy") held unquestioned naval superiority across the globe well into the 20th century.[[139]](#cite_note-The_Royal_Navy-160)

After the Napoleonic period, nationalism, a relatively new movement, became increasingly significant. This shaped much of the course of future European history. Its growth spelled the beginning of some states and the end of others, as the map of Europe changed dramatically in the hundred years following the [Napoleonic Era](/wiki/Napoleonic_Era "Napoleonic Era"). Rule by [fiefdoms](/wiki/Fief "Fief") and [aristocracy](/wiki/Aristocracy "Aristocracy") was widely replaced by national ideologies based on shared origins and culture. Bonaparte's reign over Europe sowed the seeds for the founding of the nation-states of [Germany](/wiki/Unification_of_Germany "Unification of Germany") and [Italy](/wiki/Italian_unification "Italian unification") by starting the process of consolidating city-states, kingdoms and [principalities](/wiki/Principality "Principality"). At the end of the war, Denmark was forced to cede Norway to Sweden mainly as a compensation for the loss of [Finland](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Finland "Grand Duchy of Finland") which the other coalition members agreed to, but because Norway had signed its [own constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Norway "Constitution of Norway") on 17 May 1814 [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") initiated the [Swedish–Norwegian War (1814)](/wiki/Swedish%E2%80%93Norwegian_War_(1814) "Swedish–Norwegian War (1814)"). The war was a short one taking place between 26 July – 14 August 1814 and was a Swedish victory that put [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway") into a [personal union with Sweden](/wiki/Union_between_Sweden_and_Norway "Union between Sweden and Norway"). The union was peacefully dissolved in 1905. The [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands") created as a [buffer state](/wiki/Buffer_state "Buffer state") against France dissolved rapidly with the [independence of Belgium](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution") in 1830.[[140]](#cite_note-161)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Jura_de_la_Independencia_en_la_Plaza_de_Armas_de_Santiago.jpg/250px-Jura_de_la_Independencia_en_la_Plaza_de_Armas_de_Santiago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jura_de_la_Independencia_en_la_Plaza_de_Armas_de_Santiago.jpg)

Chile, one of several [Spanish territories in South America](/wiki/Spanish_Empire "Spanish Empire"), issued a [Declaration of independence](/wiki/Chilean_Declaration_of_Independence "Chilean Declaration of Independence") in 1818

The Napoleonic wars also played a key role in the independence of the Latin American colonies from Spain and Portugal. The conflict weakened the authority and military power of Spain, especially after the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar"). There were many uprisings in [Spanish America](/wiki/Spanish_America "Spanish America"), leading to the [wars of independence](/wiki/Spanish_American_wars_of_independence "Spanish American wars of independence"). In [Portuguese America](/wiki/Portuguese_America "Portuguese America"), Brazil experienced greater autonomy as it now [served as seat of the Portuguese Empire](/wiki/Transfer_of_the_Portuguese_Court_to_Brazil "Transfer of the Portuguese Court to Brazil") and ascended politically to the [status of Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Portugal,_Brazil_and_the_Algarves "United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves"). These events also contributed to the [Portuguese Liberal Revolution](/wiki/Liberal_Revolution_of_1820 "Liberal Revolution of 1820") in 1820 and the [Independence of Brazil](/wiki/Independence_of_Brazil "Independence of Brazil") in 1822.[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeenHaynes2012chpt._8-162)

The century of relative transatlantic peace, after the Congress of Vienna, enabled the "greatest intercontinental migration in human history"[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeeling199939-163) beginning with "a big spurt of immigration after the release of the dam erected by the Napoleonic Wars."[[143]](#cite_note-164) Immigration inflows relative to the US population rose to record levels (peaking at 1.6 per cent in 1850–51)[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKeeling2007267–268-165)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] as 30 million Europeans relocated to the United States between 1815 and 1914.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones199278–79-166)

Another concept emerged from the Congress of Vienna—that of a unified Europe. After his defeat, Napoleon deplored the fact that his dream of a free and peaceful "European association" remained unaccomplished. Such a European association would share the same principles of government, system of measurement, currency and [Civil Code](/wiki/Civil_Code "Civil Code"). One-and-a-half centuries later, and after two world wars several of these ideals re-emerged in the form of the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union").

## Military legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Military legacy")]

### Enlarged scope

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Enlarged scope")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Lejeune_-_Bataille_de_Marengo.jpg/250px-Lejeune_-_Bataille_de_Marengo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lejeune_-_Bataille_de_Marengo.jpg)

*[The Battle of Marengo](/wiki/The_Battle_of_Marengo_(painting) "The Battle of Marengo (painting)")* by [Louis-François Lejeune](/wiki/Louis-Fran%C3%A7ois_Lejeune "Louis-François Lejeune"). In 1800, Bonaparte took the French Army across the Alps, eventually defeating the Austrians at [Marengo](/wiki/Battle_of_Marengo "Battle of Marengo").

Until the time of [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), European states employed relatively small armies, made up of both national soldiers and [mercenaries](/wiki/Mercenary "Mercenary"). These regulars were highly drilled, professional soldiers. [Ancien Régime](/wiki/Ancien_R%C3%A9gime "Ancien Régime") armies could only deploy small field armies due to rudimentary staffs and comprehensive yet cumbersome logistics.

Military innovators in the mid-18th century began to recognise the potential of an entire nation at war: a "nation in arms".[[146]](#cite_note-167)

The scale of warfare dramatically enlarged during the Revolutionary and subsequent Napoleonic Wars. During Europe's major pre-revolutionary war, the [Seven Years' War](/wiki/Seven_Years%27_War "Seven Years' War") of 1756–1763, few armies ever numbered more than 200,000 with field forces often numbering less than 30,000. The French innovations of separate corps (allowing a single commander to efficiently command more than the traditional command span of 30,000 men) and living off the land (which allowed field armies to deploy more men without requiring an equal increase in supply arrangements such as depots and supply trains) allowed the French republic to field much larger armies than their opponents. Napoleon ensured during the time of the French republic that separate French field armies operated as a single army under his control, often allowing him to substantially outnumber his opponents. This forced his continental opponents to also increase the size of their armies, moving away from the traditional small, well-drilled Ancien Régime armies of the 18th century to mass conscript armies.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Antoine-Jean_Gros_-_Napoleon_on_the_Battlefield_of_Eylau_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Antoine-Jean_Gros_-_Napoleon_on_the_Battlefield_of_Eylau_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Antoine-Jean_Gros_-_Napoleon_on_the_Battlefield_of_Eylau_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

Napoleon on the field of [Eylau](/wiki/Battle_of_Eylau "Battle of Eylau")

The [Battle of Marengo](/wiki/Battle_of_Marengo "Battle of Marengo"), which largely ended the War of the Second Coalition, was fought with fewer than 60,000 men on both sides. The [Battle of Austerlitz](/wiki/Battle_of_Austerlitz "Battle of Austerlitz") which ended the War of the Third Coalition involved fewer than 160,000 men. The [Battle of Friedland](/wiki/Battle_of_Friedland "Battle of Friedland") which led to peace with Russia in 1807 involved about 150,000 men.

After these defeats, the continental powers developed various forms of mass conscription to allow them to face France on even terms, and the size of field armies increased rapidly. The [Battle of Wagram](/wiki/Battle_of_Wagram "Battle of Wagram") of 1809 involved 300,000 men, and 500,000 fought at [Leipzig](/wiki/Battle_of_Leipzig "Battle of Leipzig") in 1813, of whom 150,000 were killed or wounded.

About a million French soldiers became casualties (wounded, invalided or killed), a higher proportion than in the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"). The European total may have reached 5,000,000 military deaths, including disease.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBell20077-168)[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKennedy198999–100-169)[*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

France had the second-largest population in Europe by the end of the 18th century (28 million, as compared to Britain's 12 million and Russia's 35 to 40 million).[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMcEvedyJones197841–222-170)[*[page range too broad](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] It was well poised to take advantage of the *[levée en masse](/wiki/Lev%C3%A9e_en_masse "Levée en masse")*. Before Napoleon's efforts, [Lazare Carnot](/wiki/Lazare_Carnot "Lazare Carnot") played a large part in the reorganisation of the [French Revolutionary Army](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Army "French Revolutionary Army") from 1793 to 1794—a time which saw previous French misfortunes reversed, with Republican armies advancing on all fronts.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/National_Museum_in_Poznan_-_Przej%C5%9Bcie_przez_Berezyn%C4%99.JPG/250px-National_Museum_in_Poznan_-_Przej%C5%9Bcie_przez_Berezyn%C4%99.JPG)](/wiki/File:National_Museum_in_Poznan_-_Przej%C5%9Bcie_przez_Berezyn%C4%99.JPG)

Napoleon's retreat from Russia in 1812. His *[Grande Armée](/wiki/Grande_Arm%C3%A9e "Grande Armée")* had lost about half a million men.

The French army peaked in size in the 1790s with 1.5 million Frenchmen enlisted although battlefield strength was much less. Haphazard bookkeeping, rudimentary medical support and lax recruitment standards ensured that many soldiers either never existed, fell ill or were unable to withstand the physical demands of soldiering.

About 2.8 million Frenchmen fought on land and about 150,000 at sea, bringing the total for France to almost 3 million combatants during almost 25 years of warfare.[[15]](#cite_note-John_France_2011_351-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Combat_du_29_vend%C3%A9maire_an_14.jpg/250px-Combat_du_29_vend%C3%A9maire_an_14.jpg)](/wiki/File:Combat_du_29_vend%C3%A9maire_an_14.jpg)

The [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar")

Britain had 750,000 men under arms between 1792 and 1815 as its [army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") expanded from 40,000 men in 1793[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] to a peak of 250,000 men in 1813.[[150]](#cite_note-Oxford132-171) Over 250,000 sailors served in the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"). In September 1812, Russia had 900,000 enlisted men in its [army](/wiki/Imperial_Russian_Army "Imperial Russian Army"), and between 1799 and 1815 2.1 million men served in its army. Another 200,000 served in the [Imperial Russian Navy](/wiki/Imperial_Russian_Navy "Imperial Russian Navy"). Out of the 900,000 men, the field armies deployed against France numbered less than 250,000.

There are no consistent statistics for other major combatants. [Austria's forces](/wiki/Austrian_Army_during_the_French_Revolutionary_and_Napoleonic_Wars "Austrian Army during the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars") peaked at about 576,000 (during the War of the Sixth Coalition) and had little or no naval component yet never fielded more than 250,000 men in field armies. After Britain, Austria proved the most persistent enemy of France; more than a million Austrians served during the long wars. Its large army was overall quite homogeneous and solid and in 1813 operated in Germany (140,000 men), Italy and the Balkans (90,000 men at its peak, about 50,000 men during most of the campaigning on these fronts). Austria's manpower was becoming quite limited towards the end of the wars, leading its generals to favour cautious and conservative strategies, to limit their losses.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Baschkiren_gegen_Franzosen.jpg/250px-Baschkiren_gegen_Franzosen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Baschkiren_gegen_Franzosen.jpg)

French soldiers in skirmish with [Bashkirs](/wiki/Bashkirs "Bashkirs") and [Cossacks](/wiki/Cossacks "Cossacks") in 1813

Prussia never had more than 320,000 men under arms at any time. In 1813–1815, the core of its [army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army") (about 100,000 men) was characterised by competence and determination, but the bulk of its forces consisted of second- and third-line troops, as well as [militiamen](/wiki/Landwehr "Landwehr") of variable strength. Many of these troops performed reasonably well and often displayed considerable bravery but lacked the professionalism of their regular counterparts and were not as well equipped. Others were largely unfit for operations, except sieges. During the 1813 campaign, 130,000 men were used in the military operations, with 100,000 effectively participating in the main [German campaign](/wiki/German_campaign_of_1813 "German campaign of 1813"), and about 30,000 being used to besiege isolated French garrisons.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeggiere2014-172)

[Spain's armies](/wiki/Spanish_Army_(Peninsular_War) "Spanish Army (Peninsular War)") also peaked at around 200,000 men, not including more than 50,000 guerrillas scattered over Spain. In addition the [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire"), the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Italy (Napoleonic)"), [Naples](/wiki/Army_of_the_Kingdom_of_Naples_(Napoleonic) "Army of the Kingdom of Naples (Napoleonic)") and the [Duchy of Warsaw](/wiki/Duchy_of_Warsaw "Duchy of Warsaw") each had more than 100,000 men under arms. Even small nations now had armies rivalling the size of the [Great Powers](/wiki/Great_Powers "Great Powers")' forces of past wars but most of these were poor quality forces only suitable for garrison duties. The size of their combat forces remained modest yet they could still provide a welcome addition to the major powers. The percentage of French troops in the *Grande Armée* which Napoleon led into Russia was about 50 per cent while the French allies also provided a significant contribution to the French forces in Spain. As these small nations joined the coalition forces in 1813–1814, they provided a useful addition to the coalition while depriving Napoleon of much-needed manpower.

### Innovations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Innovations")]

The initial stages of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution") had much to do with larger military forces—it became easy to mass-produce weapons and thus to equip larger forces. Britain was the largest single manufacturer of armaments in this period. It supplied most of the weapons used by the coalition powers throughout the conflicts. France produced the second-largest total of armaments, equipping its own huge forces as well as those of the [Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Confederation of the Rhine") and other allies.[[152]](#cite_note-173)

Napoleon showed innovative tendencies in his use of mobility to offset numerical disadvantages, as demonstrated in the rout of the Austro–Russian forces in 1805 in the [Battle of Austerlitz](/wiki/Battle_of_Austerlitz "Battle of Austerlitz"). The French Army redefined the role of artillery, forming independent, mobile units, as opposed to the previous tradition of attaching artillery pieces in support of troops.[[153]](#cite_note-auto1-174)

The [semaphore system](/wiki/Semaphore_line "Semaphore line") had allowed the French War-Minister, Carnot, to communicate with French forces on the frontiers throughout the 1790s. The French continued to use this system throughout the Napoleonic wars. [Aerial surveillance](/wiki/Aerial_surveillance "Aerial surveillance") was used for the first time when the French used a hot-air balloon to survey coalition positions before the [Battle of Fleurus](/wiki/Battle_of_Fleurus_(1794) "Battle of Fleurus (1794)"), on 26 June 1794.[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPalmer194181–83-175)

### Total war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Total war")]

Main article: [Total war](/wiki/Total_war "Total war")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Goya-Guerra_%2832%29.jpg/250px-Goya-Guerra_%2832%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Goya-Guerra_(32).jpg)

[Goya's](/wiki/Francisco_Goya "Francisco Goya") *[The Disasters of War](/wiki/The_Disasters_of_War "The Disasters of War")*, showing French atrocities against Spanish civilians

Historians have explored how the Napoleonic wars became total wars. Most historians argue that the escalation in size and scope came from two sources. First was the ideological clash between revolutionary/egalitarian and conservative/hierarchical belief systems. Second was the emergence of [nationalism](/wiki/Nationalism "Nationalism") in France, Germany, Spain, and elsewhere, that made these "people's wars" instead of contests between monarchs.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStokerSchneidBlanton200824,_31–32,_38-176) Bell has argued that even more important than ideology and nationalism were the intellectual transformations in the culture of war that came about through the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment").[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBell20077–13-177) One factor, he says, is that war was no longer a routine event but a transforming experience for societies—a total experience. Secondly, the military emerged in its own right as a separate sphere of society distinct from the ordinary civilian world. The French Revolution made every civilian a part of the war machine, either as a soldier through universal conscription, or as a vital cog in the home front machinery supporting and supplying the army. Out of that, says Bell, came "militarism", the belief that the military role was morally superior to the civilian role in times of great national crisis. The fighting army represented the essence of the nation's soul.[[v]](#cite_note-178) As Napoleon proclaimed, "It is the soldier who founds a Republic and it is the soldier who maintains it."[[157]](#cite_note-179) Napoleon said on his career "I closed the gulf of anarchy and brought order out of chaos. I rewarded merit regardless of birth or wealth, wherever I found it. I abolished feudalism and restored equality to all religion and before the law. I fought the decrepit monarchies of the Old Regime because the alternative was the destruction of all this. I purified the Revolution."[[158]](#cite_note-180)

## Use of military intelligence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Use of military intelligence")]

Intelligence played a pivotal factor throughout the Napoleonic Wars and could very well have changed the tide of war. The use and misuse of military intelligence dictated the course of many major battles during the Napoleonic Wars. Some of the major battles that were dictated by the use of intelligence include: The [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo"), [Battle of Leipzig](/wiki/Battle_of_Leipzig "Battle of Leipzig"), [Battle of Salamanca](/wiki/Battle_of_Salamanca "Battle of Salamanca"), and the [Battle of Vitoria](/wiki/Battle_of_Vitoria "Battle of Vitoria"). A major exception to the greater use of superior military intelligence to claim victory was the [Battle of Jena](/wiki/Battle_of_Jena "Battle of Jena") in 1806. At the Battle of Jena even Prussian superior military intelligence was not enough to counter the sheer military force of Napoleons' armies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The use of intelligence varied greatly across the major world powers of the war. [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") at this time had more supply of intelligence given to him than any French general before him. However, Napoleon was not an advocate of military intelligence at this time as he often found it unreliable and inaccurate when compared to his own preconceived notions of the enemy. Napoleon rather studied his enemy via domestic newspapers, diplomatic publications, maps, and prior documents of military engagements in the theaters of war in which he would operate. It was this stout and constant study of the enemy which made Napoleon the military mastermind of his time. Whereas, his opponents—Britain, Austria, Prussia, and Russia—were much more reliant on traditional intelligence-gathering methods and were much quicker and more willing to act on them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The methods of Intelligence during these wars were to include the formation of vast and complex networks of corresponding agents, codebreaking, and cryptanalysis. The greatest cipher to be used to hide military operations during this time was known as the [Great Paris Cipher](/wiki/Great_Cipher "Great Cipher") used by the French. However, thanks to the hard work of British codebreakers like [George Scovell](/wiki/George_Scovell "George Scovell"), the British were able to crack French ciphers and gain vast amounts of military intelligence on Napoleon and his armies.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAndrew2018-181)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

### Diplomatic espionage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Diplomatic espionage")]

French diplomat [Talleyrand](/wiki/Charles_Maurice_de_Talleyrand-P%C3%A9rigord "Charles Maurice de Talleyrand-Périgord") served as a source of intelligence for the Coalition powers against Napoleon.[[160]](#cite_note-182)[[161]](#cite_note-183) At the [Congress of Erfurt](/wiki/Congress_of_Erfurt "Congress of Erfurt") in September–October 1808, Talleyrand secretly counseled Tsar Alexander. Alexander's attitude towards Napoleon was one of apprehensive opposition. Talleyrand believed Napoleon would eventually destroy the empire he had worked to build across multiple rulers.[[162]](#cite_note-184) After his resignation in 1807 from the ministry, Talleyrand began to accept bribes from hostile powers (mainly Austria, but also Russia), to betray Napoleon's secrets.[[163]](#cite_note-Lawday-185)

Various agents of Napoleon were known such as [Madame d'Oettlinger](/wiki/Madame_d%27Oettlinger "Madame d'Oettlinger").

## In fiction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Napoleonic_Wars&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: In fiction")]

Main article: [Napoleonic Wars in fiction](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars_in_fiction "Napoleonic Wars in fiction")

See also: [List of Napoleonic Wars films](/wiki/List_of_Napoleonic_Wars_films "List of Napoleonic Wars films")

The Napoleonic Wars were a defining event of the early 19th century, and inspired many works of fiction, from then until the present day.

* [Leo Tolstoy](/wiki/Leo_Tolstoy "Leo Tolstoy")'s epic novel *[War and Peace](/wiki/War_and_Peace "War and Peace")* recounts Napoleon's wars between 1805 and 1812 (especially the disastrous 1812 invasion of Russia and subsequent retreat) from a Russian perspective.
* [Stendhal](/wiki/Stendhal "Stendhal")'s novel *[The Charterhouse of Parma](/wiki/The_Charterhouse_of_Parma "The Charterhouse of Parma")* opens with a ground-level recounting of the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") and the subsequent chaotic retreat of French forces.
* *[Les Misérables](/wiki/Les_Mis%C3%A9rables "Les Misérables")* by [Victor Hugo](/wiki/Victor_Hugo "Victor Hugo") takes place against the backdrop of the Napoleonic Wars and subsequent decades, and in its unabridged form contains an epic telling of the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo").
* *[Adieu](/wiki/Adieu_(short_story) "Adieu (short story)")* is a novella by [Honoré de Balzac](/wiki/Honor%C3%A9_de_Balzac "Honoré de Balzac") in which can be found a short description of the French retreat from Russia, particularly the [battle of Berezina](/wiki/Battle_of_Berezina "Battle of Berezina"), where the fictional couple of the story are tragically separated. Years later after imprisonment, the husband returns to find his wife still in a state of utter shock and amnesia. He has the battle and their separation reenacted, hoping the memory will heal her state.
* [William Makepeace Thackeray](/wiki/William_Makepeace_Thackeray "William Makepeace Thackeray")'s novel *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(novel) "Vanity Fair (novel)")* takes place during the 1815 Napoleonic War – one of its protagonists dies at the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo"). Thackeray states in Chapter XXX "We do not claim to rank among the military novelists. Our place is with the non-combatants. When the decks are cleared for action we go below and wait meekly." And indeed he presents no descriptions of military leaders, strategy, or combat; he describes anxious non-combatants waiting in Brussels for news.
* *[Sylvia's Lovers](/wiki/Sylvia%27s_Lovers "Sylvia's Lovers")* by [Elizabeth Gaskell](/wiki/Elizabeth_Gaskell "Elizabeth Gaskell") is set in the English home-front during the Napoleonic Wars and depicts the [impressment](/wiki/Impressment "Impressment") of sailors by roving press gangs.
* *[The Duel](/wiki/The_Duel_(short_story) "The Duel (short story)")*, a short story by [Joseph Conrad](/wiki/Joseph_Conrad "Joseph Conrad"), recounts the story based on true events of two French [Hussar](/wiki/Hussar "Hussar") officers who carry a long grudge and fight in duels each time they meet during the Napoleonic wars. The short story was adapted by director [Ridley Scott](/wiki/Ridley_Scott "Ridley Scott") into the [1977 Cannes Film Festival](/wiki/1977_Cannes_Film_Festival "1977 Cannes Film Festival")'s Best First Work award-winning film *[The Duellists](/wiki/The_Duellists "The Duellists")*.
* *[Mr Midshipman Easy](/wiki/Mr_Midshipman_Easy "Mr Midshipman Easy")* (1836), semi-autobiographical novel by Captain [Frederick Marryat](/wiki/Frederick_Marryat "Frederick Marryat"), who served as a Royal Navy officer (1806–1830) including during Napoleonic Wars, and who wrote many novels, and who was a pioneer of the Napoleonic wars sea story about the experiences of British naval officers.
* *[Le Colonel Chabert](/wiki/Le_Colonel_Chabert_(novel) "Le Colonel Chabert (novel)")* by Honoré de Balzac. After being severely wounded during the [Battle of Eylau](/wiki/Battle_of_Eylau "Battle of Eylau") (1807), Chabert, a famous colonel of the [cuirassiers](/wiki/Cuirassier "Cuirassier"), was erroneously recorded as dead and buried unconscious with French casualties. After extricating himself from his grave and being nursed back to health by local peasants, it takes several years for him to recover. When he returns to the Paris of the [Bourbon Restoration](/wiki/Bourbon_Restoration_in_France "Bourbon Restoration in France"), he discovers that his "widow", a former prostitute that Chabert made rich and honourable, has married the wealthy Count Ferraud. She has also liquidated all of Chabert's belongings and pretends not to recognise her first husband. Seeking to regain his name and monies that were wrongly given away as inheritance, he hires Derville, an attorney, to win back his money and his honour.
* A poem "[Borodino](/wiki/Borodino_(poem) "Borodino (poem)")" by [Mikhail Lermontov](/wiki/Mikhail_Lermontov "Mikhail Lermontov") describes the [Battle of Borodino](/wiki/Battle_of_Borodino "Battle of Borodino") from the perspective of the poet's uncle, a Russian officer.
* *[The Count of Monte Cristo](/wiki/The_Count_of_Monte_Cristo "The Count of Monte Cristo")* by [Alexandre Dumas, père](/wiki/Alexandre_Dumas,_p%C3%A8re "Alexandre Dumas, père") starts during the tail-end of the Napoleonic Wars. The main character, [Edmond Dantès](/wiki/Edmond_Dant%C3%A8s "Edmond Dantès"), suffers imprisonment following false accusations of Bonapartist leanings.
* The novelist [Jane Austen](/wiki/Jane_Austen "Jane Austen") lived much of her life during the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars, and two of her brothers served in the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"). Austen almost never refers to specific dates or historical events in her novels, but wartime England forms part of the general backdrop to several of them: in *[Pride and Prejudice](/wiki/Pride_and_Prejudice "Pride and Prejudice")* (1813, but possibly written during the 1790s), the local [militia](/wiki/Militia "Militia") (civilian volunteers) has been called up for home defence and its officers play an important role in the plot; in [Mansfield Park](/wiki/Mansfield_Park "Mansfield Park") (1814), Fanny Price's brother William is a [midshipman](/wiki/Midshipman "Midshipman") (officer in training) in the [Royal Navy](/wiki/Royal_Navy "Royal Navy"); and in *[Persuasion](/wiki/Persuasion_(novel) "Persuasion (novel)")* (1818), Frederick Wentworth and several other characters are naval officers recently returned from service.
* [Charlotte Brontë](/wiki/Charlotte_Bront%C3%AB "Charlotte Brontë")'s novel *[Shirley](/wiki/Shirley_(novel) "Shirley (novel)")* (1849), set during the Napoleonic Wars, explores some of the economic effects of war on rural Yorkshire.
* [Arthur Conan Doyle](/wiki/Arthur_Conan_Doyle "Arthur Conan Doyle")'s [Brigadier Gerard](/wiki/Brigadier_Gerard "Brigadier Gerard") serves as a French soldier during the Napoleonic Wars
* [Fyodor Dostoevsky](/wiki/Fyodor_Dostoevsky "Fyodor Dostoevsky")'s book *[The Idiot](/wiki/The_Idiot_(novel) "The Idiot (novel)")* had a character, General Ivolgin, who witnessed and recounted his relationship with Napoleon during the Campaign of Russia.
* [Roger Brook](/wiki/Roger_Brook "Roger Brook") is a fictional secret agent and Napoleonic Wars Era gallant, later identified as the Chevalier de Breuc, in a series of twelve novels by [Dennis Wheatley](/wiki/Dennis_Wheatley "Dennis Wheatley")
* The *[Hornblower](/wiki/Horatio_Hornblower "Horatio Hornblower")* books by [C.S. Forester](/wiki/C.S._Forester "C.S. Forester") follow the naval career of Horatio Hornblower during the Napoleonic Wars. The 1951 film "[Captain Horatio Hornblower](/wiki/Captain_Horatio_Hornblower "Captain Horatio Hornblower")" starring [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck") and [Virginia Mayo](/wiki/Virginia_Mayo "Virginia Mayo") and directed by [Raoul Walsh](/wiki/Raoul_Walsh "Raoul Walsh") is a film adaption based on Forester's series of novels. Also by C.S. Forester two novels of the Peninsular War in Spain and Portugal: "[Death to the French](/wiki/Death_to_the_French "Death to the French")" (1932, published in the United States under the title "Rifleman Dodd"), and "[The Gun](/wiki/The_Gun_(novel) "The Gun (novel)")" (1933), later made into a 1957 film, "[The Pride and the Passion](/wiki/The_Pride_and_the_Passion "The Pride and the Passion")", with [Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant"), [Frank Sinatra](/wiki/Frank_Sinatra "Frank Sinatra"), [Sophia Loren](/wiki/Sophia_Loren "Sophia Loren"), directed by [Stanley Kramer](/wiki/Stanley_Kramer "Stanley Kramer").
* [R. F. Delderfield](/wiki/R._F._Delderfield "R. F. Delderfield"), two novels about the Napoleonic Wars; *[Seven Men of Gascony](/wiki/Seven_Men_of_Gascony "Seven Men of Gascony")* (1949) about seven French infantrymen serving in a succession of Napoleonic campaigns, and *[Too Few For Drums](/wiki/Too_Few_For_Drums "Too Few For Drums")* (1964) about British soldiers cut off behind the French lines in Portugal in 1810, during the Peninsular War.
* The [Aubrey–Maturin series](/wiki/Aubrey%E2%80%93Maturin_series "Aubrey–Maturin series") of novels is a sequence of 20 [historical novels](/wiki/Historical_novel "Historical novel") by [Patrick O'Brian](/wiki/Patrick_O%27Brian "Patrick O'Brian") portraying the rise of Jack Aubrey from Lieutenant to Rear Admiral during the Napoleonic Wars. The film *[Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World](/wiki/Master_and_Commander:_The_Far_Side_of_the_World "Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World")* starring [Russell Crowe](/wiki/Russell_Crowe "Russell Crowe") and directed by [Peter Weir](/wiki/Peter_Weir "Peter Weir") is based on this series of books.
* The *[Sharpe](/wiki/Sharpe_(novel_series) "Sharpe (novel series)")* series by [Bernard Cornwell](/wiki/Bernard_Cornwell "Bernard Cornwell") stars the character Richard Sharpe, a soldier in the British Army, who fights throughout the Napoleonic Wars. It was adapted into the [*Sharpe* TV Series](/wiki/Sharpe_(TV_series) "Sharpe (TV series)") starring [Sean Bean](/wiki/Sean_Bean "Sean Bean").
* The *[Bloody Jack](/wiki/Bloody_Jack_(novel) "Bloody Jack (novel)")* book series by [Louis A. Meyer](/wiki/Louis_A._Meyer "Louis A. Meyer") is set during the Second Coalition of the Napoleonic Wars, and retells many famous battles of the age. The heroine, Jacky, meets Bonaparte.
* The Napoleonic Wars provide the backdrop for *The Emperor*, *The Victory*, *The Regency* and *The Campaigners*, Volumes 11, 12, 13 and 14 respectively of [The Morland Dynasty](/wiki/The_Morland_Dynasty "The Morland Dynasty"), a series of historical novels by the author [Cynthia Harrod-Eagles](/wiki/Cynthia_Harrod-Eagles "Cynthia Harrod-Eagles").
* The [Richard Bolitho](/wiki/Richard_Bolitho "Richard Bolitho") series by [Alexander Kent](/wiki/Douglas_Reeman "Douglas Reeman") novels portray this period of history from a naval perspective.
* G.S. Beard, author of two novels (2010) about John Fury, British naval officer during the Napoleonic Wars.
* *Napoleon's Blackguards*, a novel by Stephen McGarry, set in Spain during the Napoleonic Wars about the travails of an elite unit of Napoleon's [Irish Legion](/wiki/Irish_Legion "Irish Legion").
* Robert Challoner, author of three novels in the series about Charles Oakshott, British naval officer in Napoleonic Wars.
* [David Donachie](/wiki/David_Donachie "David Donachie")'s John Pearce series about a pressed seaman who becomes a British naval officer during the French Revolution wars and Napoleonic Wars.
* [Julian Stockwin](/wiki/Julian_Stockwin "Julian Stockwin")'s Thomas Kydd series portrays one man's journey from pressed man to Admiral in the time of the French and Napoleonic Wars
* [Simon Scarrow](/wiki/Simon_Scarrow "Simon Scarrow") – Napoleonic series. Rise of Napoleon and Wellington from humble beginnings to history's most remarkable and notable leaders. Four books in the series.
* The [Lord Ramage](/wiki/Lord_Ramage "Lord Ramage") series by [Dudley Pope](/wiki/Dudley_Pope "Dudley Pope") takes place during the Napoleonic Wars.
* [Jeanette Winterson](/wiki/Jeanette_Winterson "Jeanette Winterson")'s 1987 novel *[The Passion](/wiki/The_Passion_(novel) "The Passion (novel)")*
* [Georgette Heyer](/wiki/Georgette_Heyer "Georgette Heyer")'s 1937 novel *[An Infamous Army](/wiki/An_Infamous_Army "An Infamous Army")* recounts the fortunes of a family in the run-up to and during the course of, the [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo"). Heyer's novel is noted for its meticulous research on the progress of the battle, combining her noted period romance writing with her detailed research into regency history.
* *[The Battle](/wiki/The_Battle_(Rambaud_novel) "The Battle (Rambaud novel)")* (French: *La Bataille*) is a historical novel by the French author [Patrick Rambaud](/wiki/Patrick_Rambaud "Patrick Rambaud") that was first published in 1997 and again in English in 2000. The book describes the 1809 [Battle of Aspern-Essling](/wiki/Battle_of_Aspern-Essling "Battle of Aspern-Essling") between the French Empire under Napoleon and the Austrian Empire. The novel was awarded the [Prix Goncourt](/wiki/Prix_Goncourt "Prix Goncourt") and the [Grand Prix du roman de l'Académie française](/wiki/Grand_Prix_du_roman_de_l%27Acad%C3%A9mie_fran%C3%A7aise "Grand Prix du roman de l'Académie française") for 1997.
* In [Jasper Kent](/wiki/Jasper_Kent "Jasper Kent")'s novel *Twelve*, 1812 Russian Invasion serves as a base story for the book. In later books from The Danilov Quintet, this war is constantly mentioned.
* The *Fighting Sail* series by Alaric Bond portrays life and action aboard Royal Naval vessels during the Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars. From the lower decks to the quarterdeck Bond's detailed settings are realistic. Narratives are told not just from a commissioned officer's point of view but include varied perspectives, including warranted officers, ordinary and able seamen, marines, supernumeraries, and women aboard presenting a broader, more complete picture of the Georgian Navy.[[164]](#cite_note-186)
